4. Release the shift lock lever so the selector locks in place.

Important: Do not turn the QSTS selector while operating at full throttle. Doing so could damage the QSTS.

Trim position effect:

- Neutral “N”: Provides good overall performance for most conditions.
- Bow up: Raises the front of the watercraft while on plane. This reduces water resistance, which can improve straight-line acceleration and top speed.
- Bow down: Lowers the front of the watercraft, which can help if the watercraft begins to “porpoise,” meaning it hops up and down on the water.

<PIC>

If the watercraft starts porpoising while using a bow-up setting, return the selector to neutral or choose a bow-down position for more stable handling.,['Manual20_51', 'Manual20_52', 'Manual20_53']

The fuel meter and hour meter are both displayed in the multifunction information center on your 2005 WaveRunner. This display is used to monitor key operating information while riding, including fuel level, engine hours, speed, engine rpm, and warning indicators.

<PIC>

The fuel meter shows the approximate amount of fuel remaining in the fuel tank. It uses eight display segments, so more lit segments mean more fuel remaining. Because fuel can move around in the tank while the watercraft is accelerating, turning, or riding over waves, the display should be used as a reference rather than an exact measurement.

<PIC>

When the fuel level drops to about 13 L / 3.4 US gal / 2.9 Imp gal, the lowest two fuel segments will blink. The fuel warning indicator and warning light will also blink, and the buzzer will sound intermittently. Refuel as soon as possible if this happens.

The hour meter shows the total engine operating time since the watercraft was new. This is mainly used to help track maintenance intervals, since service items are often based on engine hours rather than calendar time alone.

<PIC>

The hour reading is retained even if the battery terminals are disconnected. If the hour meter malfunctions, the display will show “_-_” and the warning light will blink.,['Manual20_54', 'Manual20_58', 'Manual20_59']

Yes, inspecting the fuel filter and fuel tank is an important part of keeping your WaveRunner safe and reliable. Here is how to check them and what to do if you find a problem. Fuel filter inspection Your WaveRunner is equipped with a one-piece, disposable fuel filter. The filter is not intended to be cleaned or reused; it should be replaced when required. Check the fuel filter area for signs of water in the filter, fuel leakage, cracking, looseness, or damage. The fuel filter should be replaced after the initial 10 hours of operation or first month, whichever comes first. After that, it should be replaced every 200 hours or 24 months, or any time water is found in the filter. Important safety note: Do not try to replace the fuel filter yourself. If the filter is installed incorrectly, gasoline can leak, creating a serious fire or explosion hazard. If replacement is needed, have a Yamaha dealer replace it. Fuel tank inspection Inspect the fuel tank for leakage and for any indication of water in the tank or fuel system. Look around the tank area for fuel odor, wet spots, stains, damaged hoses, or anything that suggests fuel may be escaping. If water is found in the fuel system, or if the fuel tank needs to be cleaned, the watercraft should be serviced by a Yamaha dealer. Do not continue operating the WaveRunner with suspected water contamination or fuel leakage, as this can affect engine performance and may create a safety risk.,['Manual20_81', 'Manual20_82']

You can adjust the adjustable sponsons to fine-tune the WaveRunner’s handling for either better straight-line performance or stronger turning grip. Please adjust both left and right sponsons the same way so the watercraft remains balanced.

1. Remove the three mounting bolts on each sponson.

<PIC>

2. Remove both sponsons, then reinstall them in the desired position.

Important: Both sponsons must be installed at the same height/level on both sides. Do not set one side higher or lower than the other.

3. Tighten the bolts on both sponsons securely to the specified torque.

<PIC>

Tightening torque: 18 N·m, 1.8 kgf·m, or 13 ft·lb.

Position effects:

<PIC>

Up position: Reduces water resistance. This helps improve straight-ahead acceleration when the watercraft is on plane and can enhance top speed.

Down position: Gives the watercraft more “hook” in the water. This improves turning performance and cornering grip.

In short, choose the up position if you want more straight-line speed and acceleration, and choose the down position if you want sharper turning response.,['Manual20_83', 'Manual20_84', 'Manual20_70']

If weeds, grass, rope, or other debris are caught in the jet intake or around the impeller, the watercraft may cavitate. A common sign is that the engine speed rises, but thrust and acceleration decrease. If you continue operating in this condition, the engine can overheat and may be seriously damaged.

If you suspect the jet intake or impeller is clogged, beach the watercraft and inspect the area. Always stop the engine before beaching or checking the intake.

<PIC>

Warning: Before removing weeds or debris from the jet intake or impeller area, shut the engine off and remove the clip from the engine shut-off switch. Do not reach into the jet pump area while the engine can start or while parts may be rotating. Contact with rotating jet pump parts can cause severe injury or death.

To clean the intake and impeller:

1. Turn the watercraft onto its port, or left, side as shown below. Place a clean cloth, carpeting, or other soft protective material under the hull first to help prevent scratches and abrasions. Support the bow while turning the watercraft so the handlebars are not bent or damaged.

<PIC>

2. Remove any weeds or debris from around the drive shaft, impeller, pump housing, and jet thrust nozzle. Work carefully and make sure the intake grate and nozzle area are clear before operating again.

If the debris is difficult to remove, or if you cannot fully clear the intake or impeller area, have a Yamaha dealer inspect and service the watercraft.

To help prevent this from happening again, avoid operating in areas with heavy weed growth whenever possible. If you must travel through weedy water, vary the throttle between partial and full throttle instead of holding a steady low or trolling speed, because weeds tend to collect more easily at steady or very low speeds.,['Manual20_85', 'Manual20_86']

Here is how to start your jetski safely in different situations.

Before every start, attach the engine shut-off cord to your wrist, then install the clip on the engine shut-off switch. This is required before the engine can start; if the clip is removed, the engine will not start and the switch will also stop the engine if the operator falls off.

<PIC>

Once the cord and clip are correctly attached, press the green start switch to start the engine.

<PIC>

Starting and boarding in shallow water:

1. Do not start the engine in water shallower than 60 cm / 2 ft. Move the watercraft into water deep enough first, then board from the side or the rear. Starting in shallow water can pull sand, stones, or debris into the jet intake.

<PIC>

2. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your left wrist, then install the clip on the engine shut-off switch.
3. Sit securely, grip the handlebars with both hands, place both feet flat in the footwells, start the engine, check your surroundings, and then move off smoothly.

Starting from a dock:

1. Board the watercraft from the side.
2. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your left wrist, then install the clip on the engine shut-off switch.
3. Push the watercraft away from the dock before starting off. Then grip the handlebars with both hands, place both feet in the footwells, start the engine, check around you, and proceed carefully.

<PIC>

Boarding and starting with a passenger:

Important: Do not apply throttle until the passenger is fully seated, has both feet in the footwells, and is holding on securely to the operator. The passenger must stay away from the jet thrust nozzle, as high-pressure water from the nozzle can cause serious internal injury.

1. The operator should board first, sit astride the seat, attach the engine shut-off cord to the wrist, and install the clip on the engine shut-off switch.
2. Have the passenger move to the rear of the watercraft.
3. Have the passenger climb on board and sit behind the operator. Both the operator and passenger should balance the watercraft during boarding.

<PIC>

4. Before starting, confirm that the passenger’s feet are on the footwell floor and that the passenger is holding on securely.
5. Start the engine, look in all directions for boats, swimmers, and obstacles, then accelerate gradually to planing speed.

<PIC>

Important safety notes before moving off:

Always scan continuously for people, objects, swimmers, and other watercraft. Keep a safe distance and operate defensively at safe speeds. Remember that watercraft and boats do not have brakes. Also, do not release the throttle when trying to steer away from an object, because this type of watercraft needs jet thrust from the throttle to steer effectively.

<PIC>,['Manual20_44', 'Manual20_48', 'Manual20_88', 'Manual20_89', 'Manual20_90', 'Manual20_92', 'Manual20_87']

To board your jetski with a passenger in deep water, keep the craft as balanced and steady as possible, and make sure both riders are securely positioned before applying throttle.

1. The operator should board first from the rear, then sit astride the seat. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your wrist, and install the clip on the engine shut-off switch.

2. Have the passenger move to the rear of the watercraft. The passenger should approach carefully and avoid pushing the craft sideways.

3. Have the passenger climb aboard from the rear and sit on the seat behind the operator. While the passenger is getting on, both the operator and passenger should shift their weight carefully to help keep the watercraft balanced.

<PIC>

4. Before starting off, the operator should confirm that the passenger is fully seated, has both feet on the floor of the footwell, and is securely holding on to the operator.

<PIC>

5. Start the engine only after both riders are stable. Look in all directions to check for boats, obstructions, swimmers, or other hazards, then accelerate smoothly to planing speed.

<PIC>

Important safety reminder: Do not apply throttle until the passenger is seated, has both feet in the footwell, and is holding on to the operator. Serious internal injuries can occur if a person is near the jet thrust nozzle and water is forced into body cavities.

Also make sure the total load does not exceed 160 kg (353 lb), including the operator, passenger, and any cargo. If the craft feels difficult to balance at a standstill, take extra time to steady it before starting off.,['Manual20_90', 'Manual20_91', 'Manual20_92']

If the jetski is difficult to balance while standing still, use the following boarding method with the passenger helping stabilize the craft:

1. Have the passenger stay in the water and steady the jetski while the operator boards. The operator should pull himself up onto the rear platform, move into a seated position, and then balance the jetski from the seat.

<PIC>

2. Once seated, attach the engine shut-off cord/lanyard to your left wrist, then install the clip on the engine shut-off switch. Start the engine and keep it at idle speed.

3. The passenger should then pull himself up onto the rear platform, kneel down, and carefully crawl forward onto the seat as the jetski begins to accelerate. The operator should keep the craft steady and avoid sudden throttle changes.

<PIC>

4. After the passenger is seated, has both feet in the footwell, and is holding on securely to the operator, accelerate to planing speed. Once the jetski is stable on plane, reduce the throttle to the desired running speed.

<PIC>

Important: Do not apply throttle until the passenger is properly seated, has both feet on the floor of the footwell, and is holding on securely. Also, do not operate the jetski if the total weight of the operator, passenger, and cargo exceeds 160 kg (353 lb).,['jetski_06', 'jetski_07', 'jetski_08']

The maximum load for this jetski is 160 kg (353 lb). This limit includes the combined weight of everything on board: the operator, one passenger, and any cargo. The watercraft is designed for up to 2 people total only, meaning the operator plus one passenger. For safe operation, do not exceed the 160 kg (353 lb) maximum load, and do not allow more than 2 persons to ride at the same time. ,['Manual20_12']

Here is an overview of the base station for the XL490 and XL495 landline models:

**For XL490**

The XL490 base station has a handset paging/registration button.

<PIC>

**Button function:**
• Press once to find or page the handset. The handset will ring so you can locate it.
• Press and hold to put the base station into registration mode when pairing an additional handset.

**For XL495**

The XL495 base station includes the same handset paging/registration function, plus a built-in speaker and answering machine control buttons.

<PIC>

**Main parts and functions:**
• **Paging/registration button:** Press to find handsets; press and hold to enter registration mode.
• **Speaker:** Plays answering machine messages and other audio from the base station.
• **Answering machine on/off button:** Turns the answering machine on or off.
• **Delete button:** Deletes the current message during playback; press and hold to delete all old messages.
• **Volume buttons:** Decrease or increase the base station speaker volume.
• **Skip buttons:** Skip backward or forward during message playback.
• **Play/stop button:** Play recorded messages or stop message playback.

In short, the XL490 base station provides the basic find-handset and registration functions, while the XL495 adds answering machine controls and a speaker for message playback.,['Manual22_18', 'Manual22_19']

To connect the base station, please follow these steps:

1. Connect the power adapter.
   - Plug the small DC connector into the DC input jack on the bottom of the base station.
   - Plug the other end of the power adapter into a wall power socket.

<PIC>

2. Connect the telephone line cord.
   - Plug one end of the line cord into the telephone socket on the bottom of the base station.
   - Plug the other end into the telephone wall socket.

Important:
- Make sure the power supply voltage matches the voltage printed on the back or underside of the phone.
- Use only the supplied power adapter with the base station.
- The type plate is located on the bottom of the base station.

If you use DSL high-speed internet through the same telephone line, install a DSL filter between the telephone line cord and the wall telephone socket. This helps prevent line noise and caller ID problems caused by DSL interference. For details about DSL filters, please contact your DSL service provider.

In some countries or regions, you may need to attach a line adapter to the line cord first, then plug the line cord into the telephone wall socket.,['Manual22_22']

To install the handset, please follow these steps:

1. Before you begin, make sure the phone is connected properly and review the important safety precautions for the product.

2. The batteries are already installed inside the handset. Before charging the handset for the first time, pull out the battery tape from the battery door as shown below.

<PIC>

3. After removing the battery tape, place the handset on the base station to charge it. Make sure it is seated correctly in the charging position.

4. When the handset is placed correctly, you will hear a docking sound, and the handset will start charging.

Please note:
- Charge the handset for 8 hours before first use.
- It is normal for the handset to become warm while the batteries are charging.
- If you ever need to reinsert or replace the batteries, check the battery polarity carefully. Incorrect polarity may damage the product.,['Manual22_23']

To check the battery level of the landline handset, look at the battery icon on the handset display. This icon shows the current battery status.

<PIC>

The battery icon uses bars to indicate how much charge is left:

- Full battery: the icon shows three bars.
- Medium battery: the icon shows two bars.
- Low battery: the icon shows one bar.

When the handset is placed correctly on the base station or charger, the battery bars scroll or animate to show that the handset is charging. If the empty battery icon flashes, the battery is very low and the handset needs to be recharged.

<PIC>

Please note: if the batteries become completely empty, the handset will turn off. If you are on a call when the battery is almost empty, you will hear warning tones, and the call may disconnect after the warning. Place the handset back on the base station to recharge it.,['Manual22_28', 'Manual22_29']

If your handset/landline shows “Searching,” it means the handset is not currently connected to the base station. Please try the following steps:

1. Check that the base station has power.
Make sure the power adapter is firmly connected to the base station and plugged into a working wall socket. If the base station has no power, the handset cannot connect to it.

2. Move the handset closer to the base station.
The handset may be outside the operating range. Place it near the base station and wait a few moments to see whether the signal returns.

3. Register the handset to the base station if needed.
If the handset still keeps searching, it may need to be registered again. Use the paging/registration button on the base station: press it to find handsets, or press and hold it to enter registration mode.

<PIC>

After these checks, the handset should reconnect and show signal bars when it is successfully linked to the base station.,['Manual22_18']

You can identify the phone’s current status by checking the LED indicator on either the handset or the base station. The meaning depends on where the light is located and whether it is steady, off, flashing, or flashing fast.

For the handset LED indicator, you can first choose what type of LED status you want to check:

Select [Menu] > [Phone setup] > [LED status] > [Events status] or [Charge status], then press [OK] to confirm. The setting will be saved.

<PIC>

Handset LED indicator behavior:

1. Events status

- On: Audio boost is on.
- Off: The phone is in a call or in standby mode.
- Flashing: This may mean one of the following:
  - There is a new message.
  - There is a new voicemail.
  - There is a missed call.
  - The phone is muted.

2. Charging status

- On: The handset is placed on the base or charging cradle in standby mode, or audio boost is on during a call.
- Off: The handset is not on the base or charging cradle.
- Flashing: The phone is muted.

For the base station LED indicator, you can use the light behavior to check answering machine and phone activity status.

<PIC>

Base station LED indicator behavior:

- Steady on:
  - There is no new message and the memory is not full.
  - The handset is switched on.
  - The phone has entered paging mode.

- Flashing:
  - There is a new message and the memory is not full.
  - There is an incoming call.
  - The phone is recording an incoming message or announcement.
  - Messages are being played back.
  - Remote access is active, or messages are being listened to from the handset.

- Flashing fast:
  - There is no new message, but the memory is full.
  - The phone has entered registration mode.

In short, check the handset LED for call, mute, charging, missed call, voicemail, or audio boost status; check the base station LED mainly for answering machine, paging, incoming call, memory, and registration status.,['Manual22_40', 'Manual22_46']

You can identify the current base station status by checking how the LED indicator behaves. The meanings are shown below:

<PIC>

**Steady on**
This usually indicates a normal or active-ready status:
- There is no new message, and the message memory is not full.
- The handset is switched on.
- The base station has entered paging mode, for example when locating the handset.

**Flashing**
This indicates an active event or answering machine activity:
- There is a new message, and the message memory is not full.
- There is an incoming call.
- The base station is recording an incoming message or announcement.
- A message is being played back.
- Remote access is in use, or messages are being listened to from the handset.

**Flashing fast**
This usually means the base station needs attention or is in a special setup mode:
- There is no new message, but the message memory is full.
- The base station has entered registration mode for registering a handset.

So, in short: a steady LED means the base station is in a normal/ready state, a flashing LED means there is call or message activity, and a fast flashing LED means the memory may be full or the base station is in registration mode.,['Manual22_46']

To lower the roll bar on the lawn mower, follow these steps carefully:

1. Park the mower on a level surface, engage the parking brake, shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop.

2. Remove the hairpin cotters, then remove the 2 pins that secure the roll bar.

3. Lower the roll bar to the desired down position. There are 2 available down positions, as shown below:

<PIC>

4. Reinstall the 2 pins through the roll bar brackets.

5. Secure both pins with the hairpin cotters to make sure the roll bar is locked in place.

Important: Lower the roll bar only when absolutely necessary, such as when temporary overhead clearance is required.

Warning: When the roll bar is down, there is no rollover protection. Do not wear the seat belt with the roll bar lowered. Drive slowly and carefully, check overhead clearance before moving under objects such as branches, doorways, or wires, and raise the roll bar again as soon as clearance allows.,['Manual23_32']

For lawn mowers equipped with the suspension system, you can adjust the 2 rear-shock assemblies to change the ride feel and improve operator comfort. Move the rear shocks toward the softer or firmer positions based on your preference and mowing conditions.

The rear-shock slots include detent positions as reference points, but the shocks do not have to sit only in those detents; they can be positioned anywhere along the slot. For best balance and handling, always set the left and right rear-shock assemblies to the same position.

<PIC>

To adjust the rear-shock assemblies:

1. Park the mower safely, engage the parking brake, shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop.
2. Flip the quick-release pin lever downward to the unlocked position.
3. Slide the top of the rear-shock assembly left or right along the slot until you reach the desired ride stiffness.
4. Repeat the adjustment on the opposite rear-shock assembly.
5. Confirm that both the left and right rear shocks are adjusted symmetrically to the same position or matching detent reference.
6. Flip the quick-release pin lever upward to the locked position to secure each shock assembly.

Important: The left and right rear-shock assemblies must always be adjusted to the same position. Uneven settings can affect ride comfort, balance, and machine handling.,['Manual23_38']

For a lawn mower equipped with an electric deck lift, adjust the height of cut as follows:

1. Raise the mower deck by pushing up on the deck-lift switch. The switch controls deck movement: push up to raise the deck and push down to lower it.

<PIC>

2. Choose the desired cutting height on the height-of-cut bracket. Align the height-of-cut pin with the hole that matches the cutting height you want, then insert the pin securely into that hole.

<PIC>

3. Lower the mower deck by pushing down on the deck-lift switch. The deck will settle onto the selected height-of-cut pin, setting the cutting height.

Important: Whenever you change the height of cut, also adjust the anti-scalp rollers to match the new cutting height. This helps prevent the deck from scalping uneven turf and improves cut quality.,['Manual23_55', 'Manual23_56']

To load a lawn mower onto a trailer or truck safely, use a heavy-duty trailer or truck and a full-width ramp. Make sure the trailer or truck has the required brakes, lights, and markings, and check your local tie-down requirements before transport.

Before loading, confirm the ramp setup is safe:

<PIC>

1. Use only a full-width ramp; do not use separate narrow ramps for each side of the mower.
2. Keep the ramp angle at 15 degrees or less.
3. The ramp should be at least 4 times as long as the height from the ground to the trailer or truck bed. This helps keep the loading angle low and reduces the risk of tipping.

Loading steps:

1. If using a trailer, connect it securely to the towing vehicle and attach the safety chains.
2. If applicable, connect the trailer brakes and lights.
3. Lower the ramp and verify that the ramp-to-ground angle does not exceed 15 degrees.
4. Back the machine up the ramp onto the trailer or truck. When unloading, drive the machine forward down the ramp.

<PIC>

5. Once the mower is positioned, shut off the engine, remove the key, and engage the parking brake.
6. Secure the machine using straps, chains, cable, or ropes near the front caster wheels and rear bumper/tie-down points.

<PIC>

Warning: Loading a mower onto a trailer or truck increases the risk of tip-over, which can cause serious injury or death. Use extreme caution on the ramp, back the machine up the ramp, drive it forward down the ramp, and avoid sudden acceleration or sudden braking while on the ramp.,['Manual23_63', 'Manual23_64', 'Manual23_65']

To unload the lawn mower from a trailer or truck, follow these steps carefully:

1. Lower the full-width ramp and make sure it is properly positioned on level ground. The ramp angle must not exceed 15 degrees. As a guideline, the ramp should be at least 4 times as long as the height from the trailer or truck bed to the ground.

<PIC>

2. Start the machine and drive it forward slowly down the ramp. Keep the machine straight on the ramp, move at a steady low speed, and avoid sudden acceleration, braking, or turning.

<PIC>

Warning: Loading or unloading the machine onto a trailer or truck increases the risk of tip-over, which could cause serious injury or death. Use extreme caution on the ramp, use only a full-width ramp, and do not use separate individual ramps for each side of the machine.,['Manual23_63', 'Manual23_64']

To change the engine oil on your lawn mower, follow these steps. The procedure is slightly different depending on whether your machine has a Kawasaki or Kohler engine, so use the matching images for your engine type.

1. Warm the oil first
Start the engine and let it run for about 5 minutes. Warm oil flows more easily and drains more completely.

2. Position the mower for draining
Park the machine so that the oil-drain side is slightly lower than the opposite side. This helps the old oil drain out as fully as possible.

3. Secure the machine
Disengage the blade-control switch/PTO and engage the parking brake.

4. Shut everything down safely
Turn off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop before leaving the operating position.

5. Drain the old engine oil
Place a drain pan under the oil drain hose. Pull the drain hose out, route it into the pan, loosen and remove the drain plug, and allow the oil to drain completely. Reinstall and tighten the plug securely when finished.

For Kawasaki engines:
<PIC>

For Kohler engines:
<PIC>

6. Add new engine oil
Slowly pour about 80% of the specified oil amount into the filler tube first. Wait a few minutes for the oil to settle, then check the dipstick and continue adding small amounts until the level reaches the Full mark. Avoid overfilling.

For Kawasaki engines:
<PIC>

For Kohler engines:
<PIC>

7. Run and recheck
Start the engine, drive the mower to a flat, level area, shut it off, and check the oil level again. Add oil only if needed to bring the level back to the Full mark.

8. Dispose of used oil properly
Take the used oil to a recycling center or approved oil collection location.

Recommended interval: Change the engine oil every 100 hours of operation, or more often if the mower is used in dirty or dusty conditions.,['Manual23_75', 'Manual23_86', 'Manual23_76', 'Manual23_87']

To remove the lawn mower air-cleaner filters, first identify which engine is installed on your machine, as the procedure is slightly different for Kawasaki and Kohler engines.

Before starting either procedure, park the machine on a level surface, disengage the blade-control switch (PTO), engage the parking brake, shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop. This helps prevent accidental starting or contact with moving parts.

For Kawasaki engines:

1. Release the latches on the air-cleaner cover.
2. Pull the air-cleaner cover away from the air-cleaner body.
3. Clean the inside of the cover with compressed air so loose dust does not fall into the intake area.
4. Gently slide the primary filter straight out of the air-cleaner body. Avoid bumping or knocking the filter against the side of the housing, as this can shake dirt into the intake.
5. Remove the safety filter only if you are replacing it.

<PIC>

For Kohler engines:

1. Release the latches on the air cleaner and remove the air-inlet cover from the air-cleaner body.
2. Clean the air-inlet screen and the cover.
3. Reinstall the air-inlet cover and secure it with the latches.

<PIC>

4. Next, release the latches on the air cleaner and pull the air-cleaner cover off the air-cleaner body.
5. Clean the inside of the air-cleaner cover with compressed air.
6. Gently slide the primary filter out of the air-cleaner body. Avoid knocking the filter into the side of the body.
7. Remove the inner filter only if you intend to replace it.

<PIC>

Important: Do not try to clean the safety filter or inner filter. If it is dirty, replace both the inner/safety filter and the primary filter. Also, do not clean the primary filter; replace it if it is dirty, bent, or damaged. Always operate the engine with both filters and the air-cleaner cover installed to help prevent engine damage.,['Manual23_72', 'Manual23_82', 'Manual23_83']

To replace the mower belt, follow the steps below. Make sure the mower is completely shut down and all moving parts have stopped before working around the deck or belt area.

1. Park the machine on a level surface.
2. Disengage the blade-control switch (PTO).
3. Engage the parking brake.
4. Shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop before leaving the operating position.
5. Lower the mower deck to the 76 mm (3 inches) height-of-cut position.
6. Remove the belt covers. Press the cover tab down, slide the cover out, and remove it from the mower deck as shown below.

<PIC>

7. Insert a 3/8-inch ratchet into the square hole in the idler arm to relieve tension on the idler spring.
8. With the belt tension released, remove the belt from the mower-deck pulleys and from the clutch pulley.
9. Remove the belt guide from the spring-loaded idler arm.
10. Remove the old mower belt from the deck.
11. Route the new mower belt around the mower-deck pulleys and the clutch pulley under the engine. Use the belt-routing diagram below to confirm that the belt is seated correctly around each pulley and through the idler assembly.

<PIC>

In the diagram:
1. Spring
2. Clutch pulley
3. Mower belt
4. Spring-loaded idler assembly
5. Ratchet
6. Square hole in the idler arm for the ratchet

12. Reinstall the belt guide on the idler arm.
13. Using the 3/8-inch ratchet in the square hole again, reinstall the idler spring.

Note: Make sure both ends of the spring are fully seated in their anchor grooves. This is important for maintaining correct belt tension.

14. Reinstall the belt covers. Slide each cover into position, press it down, and confirm that the tab locks securely into the slot on the mower deck.

<PIC>

After installation, visually check that the belt is properly seated in all pulley grooves and that the belt covers are locked in place before operating the mower.,['Manual23_99', 'Manual23_100', 'Manual23_101']

You can use the Control Set-Up pad to change several default control settings on the over-the-range microwave, including the beep sound, clock display, display scroll speed, and defrost weight unit.



Available Control Set-Up options:
1. Sound ON/OFF control: choose Sound ON or Sound OFF.
2. Clock ON/OFF control: choose whether the clock is shown or hidden.
3. Scroll Speed control: choose Slow, Normal, or Fast display scrolling speed.
4. LBS/KG choice control: choose whether defrost weight is entered in pounds or kilograms.

General setup steps:
1. Touch the Control Set-Up pad.
2. Touch the number pad for the setting you want to change:
  • Touch 1 for Sound ON/OFF
  • Touch 2 for Clock ON/OFF
  • Touch 3 for Scroll Speed
  • Touch 4 for LBS/KG choice
3. Touch the number pad for the result you want.

For example, to change the defrost weight mode from Lbs to Kg:



1. Touch Control Set-Up.
2. Touch 4 to select the LBS/KG choice control.
3. Touch 2 to select Kg. The display will confirm the setting by showing KG.,['Manual24_9', 'Manual24_10']

You can set the over-the-range microwave’s Light Timer so the cooktop/countertop light turns on and off automatically. When set, the light turns on at the low setting at the same time every day, then turns off at the time you choose, until you change or cancel the timer.

Example: set the light to turn on at 2:00 AM and turn off at 7:00 AM.

<PIC>

Steps:
1. Touch the Light Timer pad. Press it once for AM or twice for PM.
2. Enter the time you want the light to turn on. For 2:00, touch [2], [0], [0].
3. Touch the Light Timer pad again. Press it once for AM or twice for PM.
4. Enter the time you want the light to turn off. For 7:00, touch [7], [0], [0].
5. Touch the Start pad to save the setting.

After setup, the display returns to the time of day, and the light timer will run automatically each day.

To change the light timer, repeat the same steps with the new on/off times. To cancel the timer, touch Light Timer, then touch Stop/Clear. If the light timer is currently operating and you want to turn the light off, touch the Light HI/LO/Off pad.,['Manual24_11']

Favorite Recipe allows you to save one cooking time in memory, so you can quickly start that same cooking setting again later. For example, you can save a 2-minute cooking time and recall it whenever needed.

To set Favorite Recipe for 2 minutes:

<PIC>

1. Touch the Favorite Recipe pad. The display will show: “1:30 TOUCH START OR ENTER NEW TIME”.
2. Enter the new cook time by touching the number pads [2], [0], and [0]. The display will show: “2:00 TOUCH START OR POWER”.
3. Touch the Start pad. The microwave will begin cooking, and the display will show the time counting down.

To use the saved Favorite Recipe later:

<PIC>

1. Touch the Favorite Recipe pad. The saved time will appear on the display, for example: “2:00 TOUCH START OR POWER”.
2. Touch the Start pad to begin cooking. The display will show the time counting down.

When the cook time ends, the microwave will beep four times and “END” will appear on the display.

Note: The power level defaults to High, but you can change the power level when setting the cooking instruction.,['Manual24_16', 'Manual24_17']

You can reheat food using the microwave’s Reheat sensor feature. This feature automatically senses humidity from the food and sets the reheating time for you, so you do not need to enter a cooking time or power level manually. The Reheat feature has 3 preset food categories: How to use Reheat For example, to reheat a casserole: 1. Place the food in a microwavable container and cover it loosely with a microwavable lid, waxed paper, or vented plastic wrap. 2. Touch the Reheat pad. 3. Select the food category by pressing the matching number pad: - 1 = Casserole - 2 = Dinner Plate - 3 = Soup/Sauce 4. The display will show the selected food type once, then show SENSING. The microwave will automatically calculate the remaining reheating time. 5. When reheating is finished, the microwave will beep four times and END will appear on the display. Recommended amounts For best results, use the Reheat sensor within the recommended food amounts: - Casserole: 1-4 cups - Dinner Plate: 1-2 servings - Soup/Sauce: 1-4 cups Important tips for sensor reheating Use only microwavable containers. Cover food loosely so steam can escape; do not use a tight-sealing cover, because trapped steam may cause overcooking. During the SENSING period, do not open the door or press Stop/Clear. After sensing is complete, the microwave will beep twice and the remaining time will appear. At that point, you may open the door to stir, turn, or rearrange the food if needed, then press Start again to continue. Also make sure the outside of the container and the inside of the microwave are dry before starting, since extra moisture can affect the sensor’s accuracy.,['Manual24_29', 'Manual24_30', 'Manual24_31']

Your over-the-range microwave has 3 preset Auto Defrost categories. Auto Defrost is recommended for frozen raw foods because the oven calculates the defrosting time based on the food type and weight you enter.

<PIC>

To use Auto Defrost:

1. Touch the Auto Defrost pad.
2. Select the food category:
   - Touch [1] for Meat
   - Touch [2] for Poultry
   - Touch [3] for Fish
3. Enter the food weight using the number pads. The weight should be entered in pounds and tenths of a pound.
4. Touch Start to begin defrosting.

Example: To defrost 1.2 lbs. of ground beef, touch Auto Defrost, touch [1] for Meat, enter [1] [2], then touch Start.

<PIC>

During the defrost cycle, the display will count down the remaining defrost time and show DEFROST. The microwave may beep partway through the cycle. When it beeps, open the door and turn, separate, or rearrange the food. Remove any portions that have already thawed, return the still-frozen portions to the oven, close the door, and touch Start again to continue.

When entering the weight, use the net weight of the food only, not including packaging or containers. If the food package lists the weight in ounces or fractions of a pound, convert it to pounds and tenths before entering it.

<PIC>

Helpful defrosting tips:

- Use Auto Defrost for raw frozen food only.
- Enter the food weight in pounds and tenths of a pound.
- The available weight range is 0.1 to 6.0 lbs.
- Separate pieces as they begin to thaw so they defrost more evenly.
- Shallow, rectangular packages usually defrost faster than thick blocks.
- If parts of the food begin to warm or cook too quickly, you may shield those areas with small pieces of aluminum foil, but the foil must not touch the oven walls.
- For best results, place food on a microwave-safe roasting rack so thawed juices do not warm or cook the food during defrosting.
- If the food has been outside the freezer for up to 20 minutes, enter a slightly lower weight to reduce the defrosting time.,['Manual24_32', 'Manual24_33', 'Manual24_34']

The grease filter on an over-the-range microwave should be removed and cleaned regularly, at least once a month. Cleaning it often helps prevent grease buildup on the hood area and keeps the vent working properly.

1. Remove the grease filter
Slide the filter to one side first. Then pull it downward and push it toward the opposite side. Once it clears the slot, the filter will drop out.

<PIC>

2. Clean and dry the filter
Soak the grease filter in hot water mixed with a mild detergent, then wash away the grease. Rinse it thoroughly with clean water and shake off excess water. Allow it to dry completely before reinstalling.

Avoid using ammonia or harsh/corrosive cleaners, as they can damage the aluminum filter. Also note that washing aluminum filters in a dishwasher may cause the filter to darken.

<PIC>

3. Reinstall the filter
Slide the filter back into the side slot, then push it upward and toward the center of the microwave until it locks securely in place.

<PIC>

Important: Do not operate the oven hood or vent without the grease filter installed, as this may increase the risk of personal injury or property damage.,['Manual24_47', 'Manual24_48', 'Manual24_49']

If your over-the-range microwave is vented indoors, the charcoal filter helps remove cooking odors from the recirculated air. It should be replaced every 6 to 12 months, or more often if you cook frequently or notice lingering odors. Please note that the charcoal filter cannot be washed or reused.

1. Disconnect power first. Unplug the microwave, or turn off power at the main power supply/circuit breaker before starting.

2. Remove the vent grille screws. Locate and remove the two vent grille mounting screws at the top front of the microwave. These are the two middle screws shown below.

<PIC>

3. Remove the vent grille. Carefully tip the grille forward from the top, then lift it out and set it aside.

4. Take out the old charcoal filter. Pull the existing filter out from the opening at the top of the microwave.

<PIC>

5. Install the new charcoal filter. Slide the replacement filter into the same opening. Make sure it sits at the angled position shown in the image; this angle helps the filter seat correctly in the vent area.

<PIC>

6. Reinstall the vent grille. Slide the bottom of the grille back into place first, then push the top inward until it snaps into position. Reinstall the two mounting screws.

7. Restore power. Turn the power back on or plug the microwave back in. If the clock was reset, set the clock again before normal use.,['Manual24_50', 'Manual24_51', 'Manual24_52']

To replace the oven light in an over-the-range microwave, follow these steps carefully:

1. Unplug the microwave or turn off power at the main power supply before starting.

2. Remove the vent cover mounting screws. These are the two middle screws located along the top vent cover.

3. Tip the vent cover forward, then lift it out to remove it.

<PIC>

4. Locate and remove the bulb holder. The bulb holder is secured inside the opening behind the vent cover.

<PIC>

5. Lift up the bulb holder and pull it forward enough to access the bulb.

<PIC>

6. Replace the old bulb with a 30- or 40-watt appliance bulb.

7. Reinstall the bulb holder securely in its original position.

8. Reinstall the vent cover: slide the bottom of the cover into place first, then push the top until it snaps into position. Replace the mounting screws.

9. Plug the microwave back in or turn the power back on at the main power supply.

Important safety note: To avoid electrical shock, severe injury, or death, do not remove the outer case of the microwave at any time. The outer case should only be removed by an authorized service technician.

<PIC>,['Manual24_54', 'Manual24_55', 'Manual24_56', 'Manual24_58']

This motherboard provides three PCI Express 3.0 x16 physical slots for PCIe graphics cards or other PCIe expansion cards that comply with PCI Express specifications. The available operating bandwidth depends on how many VGA/PCIe cards are installed.

<PIC>

You can set or use the PCI Express 3.0 x16 slots as follows:

1. Single VGA/PCIe card
Install the card in the PCIe 3.0 x16_1 slot, which is the gray slot. In this configuration, the slot runs at x16 speed. This is the recommended slot for a single graphics card to obtain the best performance.

2. Dual VGA/PCIe cards
Install the cards in PCIe 3.0 x16_1 and PCIe 3.0 x16_2. In this configuration, both slots run at x8 speed.

3. Triple VGA/PCIe cards
Install the cards in PCIe 3.0 x16_1, PCIe 3.0 x16_2, and PCIe 3.0 x16_3. In this configuration, PCIe 3.0 x16_1 and PCIe 3.0 x16_2 run at x8 speed, while PCIe 3.0 x16_3 runs at x4 speed.

If you need to adjust the PCIe link generation in BIOS, enter BIOS Setup and go to:
Advanced > System Agent (SA) Configuration > PEG Port Configuration > PCIEx16_1 / PCIEx16_2 Link Speed

Available options are: [Auto], [Gen1], [Gen2], and [Gen3]. In most cases, [Auto] is recommended unless you need to force a specific PCIe generation for compatibility.

Important notes:
- Turn off the system and unplug the power cord before installing or removing any expansion card.
- For a single graphics card, use the PCIe 3.0 x16_1 gray slot for better performance.
- When using multiple graphics cards, make sure the power supply can provide sufficient power for the whole system.
- For better cooling with multiple graphics cards, connect chassis fans to the CHA_FAN1/2/3 connectors and ensure good airflow inside the case.,['Manual25_31']

Before changing any motherboard jumper settings, please power down the system completely and take these precautions:

• Unplug the power cord from the wall socket before touching any motherboard component.
• Make sure the ATX power supply is switched off, or disconnect the power cord from the power supply.
• To avoid static electricity damage, touch a safely grounded metal object or use a grounded wrist strap before handling components.
• Hold components by their edges and avoid touching ICs, contacts, or circuitry.

The motherboard includes two jumper-related settings: Clear RTC RAM and CPU Over Voltage.

1. Clear RTC RAM jumper: 2-pin CLRTC

This jumper is used to clear the CMOS/RTC RAM. It resets stored system setup information such as date, time, BIOS settings, and system passwords.

<PIC>

To clear the RTC RAM:

1. Turn off the computer and unplug the power cord.
2. Locate the 2-pin CLRTC header on the motherboard.
3. Use a metal object, such as a screwdriver, to short the two pins briefly.
4. Plug the power cord back in and turn on the computer.
5. During boot, hold the <Del> key to enter BIOS setup, then re-enter or adjust the required BIOS settings.

If the system still does not reset correctly, remove the onboard button-cell battery, short the two CLRTC pins again, and then reinstall the battery after the CMOS data has been cleared.

Note: If the system hangs because of overclocking, you usually do not need to clear the RTC RAM. Shut down and reboot the system, and the CPU Parameter Recall feature can automatically restore the CPU-related settings to default values.

2. CPU Over Voltage jumper: 3-pin CPU_OV

The CPU_OV jumper is used for CPU overvoltage control, mainly for overclocking. It allows a higher CPU voltage range depending on the installed CPU.

<PIC>

CPU_OV jumper settings:

• Pins 1-2 shorted: Disable / default setting. Use this for normal operation.
• Pins 2-3 shorted: Enable. This allows additional CPU voltage settings for overclocking.

For regular system use, keep the jumper on pins 1-2. Only move it to pins 2-3 if you specifically need higher CPU voltage for overclocking and understand the risks, because incorrect voltage settings may cause instability or damage components.,['Manual25_33', 'Manual25_34']

To set up the motherboard rear panel connectors, connect each external device to the matching port on the rear I/O panel. Before connecting or removing cables, shut down the computer when needed and make sure the power cable and device cables are handled safely.

<PIC>

The rear panel ports are arranged as follows:

1. PS/2 Mouse/Keyboard combo port: Connects a PS/2 mouse or PS/2 keyboard.
2. DisplayPort: Connects DisplayPort-compatible displays or devices.
3. VGA port: A 15-pin port for a VGA monitor or other VGA-compatible device.
4. LAN (RJ-45) port: Connects the system to a Gigabit Local Area Network through a network hub, switch, or router.
5. Center/Subwoofer port, orange: Connects the center speaker or subwoofer.
6. Rear Speaker Out port, black: Connects rear speakers for 4.1-channel, 5.1-channel, or 7.1-channel audio.
7. Line In port, light blue: Connects external audio sources such as a tape player, CD player, DVD player, or similar device.
8. Line Out port, lime: Connects headphones or speakers. In 4.1, 5.1, and 7.1-channel setups, this port works as Front Speaker Out.
9. Microphone port, pink: Connects a microphone.
10. Optical S/PDIF Out port: Connects to amplified speakers, headphones, or S/PDIF-compatible audio devices.
11. USB 3.1 Type-A port: Connects USB 3.1 Type-A devices.
12. USB 3.1 Type-C port: Connects USB 3.1 Type-C mobile or peripheral devices.
13. USB 3.0 ports 3-6: Connect USB 3.0 devices.
14. DVI-D port: Connects DVI-D compatible display devices.
15. HDMI port: Connects HDMI displays or devices. It is HDCP compliant for protected HD content playback.
16. USB 2.0 ports 13 and 14: Connect USB 2.0 or USB 1.1 devices.

For the LAN port, use the two LEDs beside the RJ-45 connector to check link and speed status:

<PIC>

<PIC>

LAN LED meanings:

Activity/Link LED:
- Off: No network link.
- Orange: Linked.
- Orange blinking: Data activity.
- Orange blinking then steady: Ready to wake up from S5 mode.

Speed LED:
- Off: 10 Mbps connection.
- Orange: 100 Mbps connection.
- Green: 1 Gbps connection.

For audio devices, connect speakers according to your speaker configuration:

<PIC>

Audio port usage by configuration:

- 2-channel headset/speaker: Use the lime Line Out port for audio output and the pink port for microphone input.
- 4.1-channel audio: Use lime for Front Speaker Out and black for Rear Speaker Out.
- 5.1-channel audio: Use lime for Front Speaker Out, black for Rear Speaker Out, and orange for Center/Subwoofer.
- 7.1-channel audio: Use lime for Front Speaker Out, black for Rear Speaker Out, orange for Center/Subwoofer, and Optical S/PDIF Out as Side Speaker Out.

Additional notes:

- For best speed and performance, connect USB 3.0 devices to the USB 3.0 ports.
- USB 3.0 device behavior may depend on the operating system setting and controller mode.
- DVI-D cannot output an RGB signal to a CRT display and is not compatible with DVI-I.
- Multi-display output support may vary depending on the operating environment and display port combination.,['Manual25_35', 'Manual25_36', 'Manual25_37', 'Manual25_38']

You can configure the motherboard’s onboard lighting in either of the following ways:

1. From BIOS: enter BIOS Advanced Mode, then go to Advanced > Onboard Devices Configuration.
2. From Windows: use the LED Control app in Ai Suite 3.

There are two configurable onboard lighting areas: the Model name LEDs and the SupremeFX LED.

Model name LEDs

The Model name LEDs are five small LEDs located below the printed motherboard model name. They are used to highlight the model name area on the board.

<PIC>

To change the Model name LED setting in BIOS, go to:
Advanced > Onboard Devices Configuration > Model Name LED Lighting

Available options:
- Disabled: turns off the Model name LEDs
- Still Mode: keeps the LEDs steadily lit
- Breathing Mode: makes the LEDs blink intermittently

<PIC>

SupremeFX LED

The SupremeFX LED is located around the audio separation area of the motherboard. Besides providing a lighting effect, it visually outlines the separation between the audio components and the rest of the motherboard.

<PIC>

To change the SupremeFX LED setting in BIOS, go to:
Advanced > Onboard Devices Configuration > SupremeFX LED Lighting

Available options:
- Disabled: turns off the SupremeFX LED
- Still Mode: keeps the LED steadily lit
- Breathing Mode: makes the LED blink intermittently
- Flowing Mode: creates a flowing lighting effect

<PIC>

Please note: the Standby Power LED is not a customizable lighting effect. It is an indicator that lights up when the system is ON, in sleep mode, or in soft-off mode. Before removing or installing any motherboard component, shut down the system and unplug the power cable.

<PIC>,['Manual25_55', 'Manual25_56', 'Manual25_57', 'Manual25_58', 'Manual25_54']

You can update the motherboard BIOS file using one of the following methods: EZ Update, EZ Flash 3, or BIOS Updater. We generally recommend using EZ Flash 3 if you want to update directly from BIOS, or EZ Update if you prefer updating from within the operating system.

Before updating, please save a copy of the original motherboard BIOS file to a USB flash drive if possible, so you have a backup for future recovery.

1. Update BIOS with EZ Update

EZ Update is a utility in AI Suite 3 that can automatically update your motherboard software, drivers, and BIOS version. It can also manually update a BIOS file that you have already downloaded, and it allows you to select a custom boot logo shown during POST.

To open it, launch AI Suite 3, then click EZ Update on the main menu bar.

<PIC>

In the EZ Update screen, you can:
- Click Check Now! to search online for available driver, software, firmware, or BIOS updates.
- Click the browse button (...) to select a BIOS file saved on your computer.
- Click Update to start the update process.

Please note that EZ Update requires an Internet connection through your network or Internet service provider if you want it to check for updates online.

2. Update BIOS with EZ Flash 3

EZ Flash 3 allows you to update the BIOS directly from the BIOS environment, without using an operating system utility. This method supports updating by USB or by Internet.

<PIC>

To update the BIOS using EZ Flash 3:

1. Enter the BIOS setup program and switch to Advanced Mode.
2. Go to the Tool menu.
3. Select EZ Flash 3 Utility, then press <Enter> to launch it.
4. Choose either by USB or by Internet.

Update via USB:

a. Prepare a USB flash drive containing the latest BIOS file.
b. Insert the USB flash drive into a USB port.
c. Select by USB.
d. Press <Tab> to switch to the Drive field.
e. Use the Up/Down arrow keys to select the USB flash drive, then press <Enter>.
f. Press <Tab> to switch to the Folder Info field.
g. Use the Up/Down arrow keys to select the BIOS file, then press <Enter> to start the update.

Update via Internet:

a. Select by Internet.
b. Use the Left/Right arrow keys to choose the Internet connection method, then press <Enter>.
c. Follow the onscreen instructions to complete the update.

After the update is complete, reboot the system.

Important notes for EZ Flash 3:
- The USB flash drive must be formatted as FAT32 or FAT16 and should use a single partition.
- Check your Internet connection before updating by Internet.
- Do not shut down or reset the system while the BIOS is being updated, as this may cause boot failure.
- After updating, load the BIOS default settings for better compatibility and stability. You can do this by selecting Load Optimized Defaults under the Exit menu.

3. Update BIOS with BIOS Updater

BIOS Updater is used to update the BIOS in a DOS environment. To use this method, prepare a USB flash drive with the latest BIOS file and BIOS Updater utility. The USB flash drive must be formatted as FAT32 or FAT16 with a single partition, because NTFS is not supported in the FreeDOS environment.

Basic process:

1. Save the latest BIOS file and BIOS Updater utility to the USB flash drive.
2. Boot the system into DOS.
3. At the FreeDOS prompt, switch to the USB drive.
4. Run the command: bupdater /pc /g
5. Select the BIOS file in the BIOS Updater screen.
6. Confirm the update when prompted.
7. Restart the computer after the update is complete.

During this process, do not shut down or reset the system. After the update, enter BIOS and load Load Optimized Defaults under the Exit menu to help ensure system compatibility and stability.,['Manual25_60', 'Manual25_61']

You can create a RAID array on this motherboard by using the onboard Intel chipset with Serial ATA drives connected to the Intel SATA 6.0 Gb/s connectors. The Intel Z170 chipset supports RAID 0, RAID 1, RAID 5, and RAID 10 through these SATA ports.



Before starting, please check the following:
1. Connect all drives that will be used for RAID to the Intel SATA 6.0 Gb/s connectors on the motherboard.
2. Back up any important data first, because creating a RAID array may erase data on the selected drives.
3. Make sure the drives do not already belong to another RAID volume.
4. In BIOS, the SATA mode is set to AHCI by default. To create a RAID set, set SATA Mode Selection to RAID Mode. You can find this under Advanced > SATA Configuration > SATA Mode Selection.

To create RAID through EZ Tuning Wizard:

1. Enter the BIOS, then press F11 on your keyboard, or click EZ Tuning Wizard from the BIOS screen.
2. Select RAID, then click Yes to enable RAID mode.
3. The available storage drives will be displayed. Confirm that the correct drives are listed, then click Next.



4. Select the storage purpose for the RAID array, then click Next:
• Easy Backup: used for data redundancy, such as RAID 1 or RAID 10.
• Super Speed: used for higher performance, such as RAID 0 or RAID 5.



5. If you choose Easy Backup, select one of the following RAID types, then click Next:
• Easy Backup (RAID 1): mirrors data across drives for better data protection.
• Easy Backup (RAID 10): combines mirroring and striping, but it is available only when four HDDs are connected.



6. If you choose Super Speed, select one of the following RAID types, then click Next:
• Super Speed (RAID 0): improves performance by striping data across drives, but it does not provide redundancy.
• Super Speed (RAID 5): provides both improved performance and fault tolerance, and requires enough drives to support RAID 5.



7. After selecting the RAID type, click Yes to continue with the RAID setup.
8. When the RAID setup is complete, click Yes to exit the setup, then click OK to restart the system.

After the system restarts, the RAID configuration should be active. If you plan to install an operating system on the RAID volume, make sure the OS installer can detect the RAID array and load the required Intel storage driver if needed.,['Manual25_48', 'Manual25_72', 'Manual25_73', 'Manual25_74', 'Manual25_75']

You can install OS 7 on a 100 Series motherboard by using a SATA optical drive to load the USB 3.0 driver from the support DVD, while using a USB optical drive or USB storage device as the OS installation source.

Before starting, prepare the following items:
• 1 x support DVD
• 1 x OS 7 installation source
• 1 x SATA ODD
• 1 x USB device, either a USB ODD or USB storage device

If you use a USB storage device, it should have at least 8 GB capacity. Formatting the USB storage device before use is recommended, so please back up any important files first.

Steps:

1. Prepare the OS 7 installation source:
   • If using a USB ODD, insert the OS 7 installation DVD into the USB ODD.
   • If using a USB storage device, copy all files from the OS 7 installation DVD to the USB storage device on another working computer.

2. Connect the USB ODD or USB storage device to the 100 Series platform.

3. Insert the support DVD into the SATA ODD connected to the motherboard. The SATA ODD is used so the system can automatically load the required USB 3.0 driver during OS 7 installation.

4. Power on the system. During POST, press F8 to open the boot device selection screen.

<PIC>

5. In the boot menu, select the USB ODD or USB storage device that contains the OS 7 installation files, then press Enter to start the installation.

6. During the installation startup process, the USB 3.0 driver will be loaded automatically from the support DVD in the SATA ODD.

<PIC>

7. When the “Setup is starting...” screen appears, it indicates that the USB 3.0 driver has been loaded correctly.

8. Follow the on-screen instructions to complete the OS 7 installation.

Note: For OS 7 installation on a 100 Series platform, loading the USB 3.0 driver is important because USB keyboard, mouse, or USB installation media may not work properly during setup without the required driver.,['Manual25_87', 'Manual25_88']

To secure the motherboard to the chassis, first make sure the system is powered off and the power cord is unplugged. Place the motherboard into the chassis in the correct orientation: the side with the external I/O ports should face the rear of the chassis.

<PIC>

After the board is aligned with the chassis standoffs, install nine screws into the screw holes marked by the red circles in the image. Tighten each screw evenly until it is secure.

Please do not over-tighten the screws, as excessive force may damage the motherboard. Also ensure that the motherboard is properly supported by the chassis standoffs before tightening the screws.,['Manual25_6']

Before preparing to install the Central Processing Unit (CPU), please complete the following checks and safety precautions to help avoid socket damage, static electricity damage, or component failure.

<PIC>

This motherboard uses a surface-mount LGA1151 CPU socket, designed for compatible 6th Generation processors. Before installing the CPU, make sure all power cables are unplugged.

Key CPU socket precautions:

• Use only a CPU designed for the LGA1151 socket. Do not install CPUs designed for LGA1150, LGA1155, or LGA1156 sockets, as they are not compatible and may damage the socket.
• Before installation, check that the PnP cap is still installed on the CPU socket and that the socket contacts are not bent.
• If the PnP cap is missing, or if you notice damage to the PnP cap, socket contacts, or motherboard components, contact your retailer immediately before proceeding.
• Keep the PnP cap after the CPU is installed. It may be required if the motherboard needs to be returned for service.
• Damage caused by incorrect CPU installation or removal, incorrect handling of the PnP cap, or damage to the socket contacts is not covered by the product warranty.

Also take the following precautions before touching the motherboard or any installed component:

<PIC>

• Unplug the power cord from the wall socket before touching any component.
• Use a grounded wrist strap, or touch a safely grounded metal object such as the power supply case, to discharge static electricity before handling parts.
• Hold components by their edges, and avoid touching ICs, socket contacts, circuitry, or connector pins.
• If you remove any component, place it on a grounded antistatic pad or in the antistatic bag that came with the component.
• Before installing or removing the CPU or any other component, confirm that the ATX power supply is switched off or that the power cord is detached from the power supply.

After these checks are complete, you can proceed with opening the CPU socket and installing the CPU carefully, making sure the CPU orientation matches the socket alignment marks.,['Manual25_9', 'Manual25_5']

You can check the motherboard’s system memory information in two main ways: by confirming the motherboard memory specifications and slot layout, and by checking the installed memory details in BIOS.

For this motherboard, the system memory uses four DDR4 DIMM slots. The slots are labeled DIMM_A1, DIMM_A2, DIMM_B1, and DIMM_B2. Please note that DDR4 memory has a different notch position from DDR, DDR2, and DDR3 memory, so DDR/DDR2/DDR3 modules must not be installed into these DDR4 slots.

<PIC>

Supported memory specifications:
• Memory type: DDR4 DIMM
• Number of slots: 4 DIMM slots
• Maximum capacity: Up to 64 GB total
• Supported module capacities: 2 GB, 4 GB, 8 GB, and 16 GB
• Supported module type: Unbuffered, non-ECC DDR4 DIMMs
• Supported frequency range: DDR4 2133 MHz up to 3400 MHz O.C., depending on the CPU and memory module

For best compatibility, install the memory modules according to the recommended slot configuration shown below.

<PIC>

Recommended memory installation:
• 1 DIMM: Install the module in DIMM_A2 for better compatibility. The system will run in single-channel mode.
• 2 DIMMs: Install the modules in DIMM_A2 and DIMM_B2. This enables dual-channel mode and is the preferred two-module configuration.
• 4 DIMMs: Install modules in all four slots: DIMM_A1, DIMM_A2, DIMM_B1, and DIMM_B2. This uses two pairs of dual-channel memory.

You may use different memory sizes between Channel A and Channel B, but for best stability and performance, it is recommended to use memory modules from the same kit, with the same capacity, speed, CAS latency, and version. If the capacities are different, the system will map the matching portion for dual-channel operation, while any extra capacity from the larger channel will run in single-channel mode.

You can also check the currently detected memory directly in BIOS. Enter BIOS and open the Main menu; it provides an overview of the basic system information, including installed memory information.

<PIC>

For more detailed memory module information, go to Tool > SPD Information in BIOS. There you can select a DIMM slot, such as DIMM_A1, DIMM_B1, DIMM_A2, or DIMM_B2, and view the Serial Presence Detect information for the memory module installed in that slot.

Additional notes:
• A DIMM voltage below 1.65 V is recommended to help protect the CPU.
• If 4 GB or more memory is installed while using a 32-bit operating system, the usable memory may be about 3 GB or less. A 64-bit operating system is recommended for 4 GB or more memory.
• Some high-frequency or overclocking memory may run at a lower default frequency based on SPD settings. You can adjust memory frequency from the Ai Tweaker menu if needed.
• For the latest tested compatible memory modules, please check the latest QVL on the official support website.,['Manual25_18', 'Manual25_19', 'Manual25_78']

The Serial port connector, marked as the 10-1 pin COM header on the motherboard, is an internal connector used to add a traditional serial COM port to the system. It is not the same as the rear I/O ports; instead, it requires a separate serial port module/bracket.

To use it, connect the serial port module cable to the COM header on the motherboard, then install the DB-9 serial port bracket into an available slot opening at the back of the chassis.

<PIC>

As shown in the diagram, the COM connector is located along the bottom edge of the motherboard. The enlarged pinout shows the 10-1 pin layout, with one missing pin used as a key to help prevent incorrect cable orientation. The pin assignments are:

- PIN 1: DCD
- RXD
- TXD
- DTR
- GND
- DSR
- RTS
- CTS
- RI

Please note that the COM module/bracket is purchased separately. Make sure the module cable matches the motherboard's 10-1 pin COM header layout before connecting it.

You can also enable or configure the serial port in BIOS:

Go to Advanced > Onboard Devices Configuration > Serial Port Configuration.

Available options include:

- Serial Port [On/Off]: Enables or disables the onboard serial COM port.
- Change Settings: Selects the serial port base address and IRQ. Available settings include:
  - IO=3F8h; IRQ=4
  - IO=2F8h; IRQ=3
  - IO=3E8h; IRQ=4
  - IO=2E8h; IRQ=3

In most cases, keeping the default setting IO=3F8h; IRQ=4 is suitable unless your connected serial device or operating system requires a different COM resource setting.,['Manual25_39']

The motherboard includes an internal TPM header listed as **“TPM connector (14-1 pin TPM)”** under the internal connectors section.

This connector is used to install a compatible **Trusted Platform Module (TPM)**. A TPM helps securely store encryption keys, digital certificates, passwords, and other sensitive data. It can also improve platform integrity, protect digital identities, and enhance system/network security features.

<PIC>

The image shows both the physical location of the TPM header on the motherboard and its 14-1 pin layout. The pin assignments are:

**Top row:** F_CLKRUN, F_SERIRQ, F_FRAME#, F_LAD3, F_LAD2, F_LAD1, F_LAD0, with **PIN 1** marked.

**Bottom row:** +3VSB, S_PCIRST#_TBD, key space, GND, C_PCICLK_TPM, +3V, +3V.

When connecting a TPM module, please align the connector carefully with the keyed/missing-pin position and PIN 1 marking to avoid incorrect installation. Before installing or removing any motherboard component, turn off the system, unplug the power cord, and use anti-static precautions such as touching a grounded metal object or wearing a grounded wrist strap.,['Manual25_40']

The Thermal sensor connector, labeled as the 2-pin T_SENSOR or T_SENSOR1 header, is used to connect a thermistor cable so the motherboard can monitor the temperature of specific components or nearby devices inside the system, such as areas around storage devices, graphics cards, or other heat-sensitive zones.

<PIC>

As shown in the diagram, the T_SENSOR1 connector is located along the lower edge of the motherboard near the right side. It has two pins with the following pin definition:

- Pin 1: SENSOR IN
- Adjacent pin: GND

To use it, connect a compatible 2-pin thermistor cable to this header, then place the sensor end near the component or area whose temperature you want to monitor. After connection, the detected temperature can be viewed in the BIOS Monitor menu as T_Sensor Temperature, and it can also be selected as a temperature source for chassis fan control in Q-Fan settings, if supported by your configuration.

Please power off the system and unplug the power cord before connecting or removing the sensor cable. Also make sure the connector is aligned correctly and avoid placing jumper caps or unrelated cables on this header. The thermistor cable is purchased separately.,['Manual25_43']

To set Quick Release (QR/QPR) on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, wait until the pressure cooking program has finished, then press the quick release button straight down until it clicks and locks into the Vent position.



Once the button is locked in the Vent position, steam will release from the top of the steam release valve. A strong jet of steam is normal during Quick Release, so keep your hands, face, and any exposed skin away from the steam release valve while it is venting.



When all pressure has been released, the float valve will drop down into the lid. Only open the lid after the float valve has fully dropped. Never try to force the lid open while the float valve is raised.

Important: Do not use Quick Release for fatty, oily, thick, or high-starch foods, such as stews, chilli, pasta, oatmeal, or congee, or for foods that expand during cooking, such as beans and grains. For these foods, use Natural Release instead to reduce the risk of splattering or clogging.,['multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02']

To set the pressure cooking lid on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, make sure you are using the pressure cooking lid, not the air fryer lid. The pressure cooking lid is the one with the steam release valve, float valve, quick release button, and lid position marker.

<PIC>

Follow these steps to close and set the pressure cooking lid correctly:

1. Hold the lid by the handle and align the lid position marker with the matching open-position symbol on the cooker base.
2. Lower the lid straight down onto the lid track, making sure it sits evenly on the cooker base.
3. Turn the lid clockwise until the lid marker lines up with the closed/locked symbol on the cooker base.

<PIC>

When the lid is fitted correctly, the cooker will play a jingle, and the display should show OFF while the appliance is in standby.

Please note: the pressure cooking lid automatically sets to Seal when it is closed. Once the cooker pressurizes, the lid locks for safety. Do not try to remove or force open the lid while the float valve is raised. Wait until all pressure has been released and the float valve has dropped before opening the lid.,['Manual30_11', 'Manual30_13']

To install or reset the float valve on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, please use the pressure cooking lid and follow these steps:

1. From the top of the pressure cooking lid, drop the narrow end of the float valve into the float valve hole. The flat end of the float valve should remain visible on the top of the lid.

<PIC>

2. Place one finger on the flat top of the float valve to hold it in place, then carefully turn the lid over.

3. On the underside of the lid, firmly push the silicone cap onto the bottom of the float valve until it is securely attached. This silicone cap helps the float valve seal properly during pressure cooking.

<PIC>

4. Before cooking, check that both parts are installed correctly: the float valve should move up and down freely, and the silicone cap should stay firmly attached when the lid is turned over.

Important safety notes:
- Do not use the pressure cooker unless both the float valve and silicone cap are properly installed in the pressure cooking lid.
- Do not touch the float valve while the appliance is in use.
- If the float valve is raised, the cooker is still pressurized. Do not try to open the lid until the float valve has dropped fully.,['Manual30_36', 'Manual30_37']

The anti-block shield is an important safety part on the underside of the pressure cooking lid. Its job is to help stop food particles from entering or clogging the steam release pipe, which supports proper steam release and pressure regulation during pressure cooking.

<PIC>

Before using the pressure cooking lid, please check that the steam release valve, steam release pipe, anti-block shield, and float valve are clean and free from clogs. This is especially important after cooking foods that foam, expand, or leave residue, such as rice, beans, pasta, oatmeal, soups, stews, or thick sauces. A blocked steam path can affect pressure control and may lead to injury or property damage.

To remove the anti-block shield for cleaning:
1. Hold the pressure cooking lid firmly, similar to holding a steering wheel.
2. Use your thumbs to press firmly against the side of the anti-block shield.
3. Press toward the side of the lid and upward until the shield pops off the prongs underneath.

<PIC>

After removing it, clean the anti-block shield and the area around the steam release pipe. Make sure all food particles and residue are cleared away, then allow the parts to dry before reinstalling.

To reinstall the anti-block shield:
1. Position the anti-block shield over the prongs on the underside of the lid.
2. Press down firmly until it snaps securely into place.
3. Check that it is seated properly before pressure cooking.

<PIC>

Do not use the pressure cooker without the anti-block shield installed. It is required for safe pressure cooking and helps the appliance regulate pressure correctly.,['Manual30_19', 'Manual30_34', 'Manual30_35']

The steam release valve is controlled by the quick release button on the pressure cooking lid. This button determines whether the cooker stays sealed for pressure cooking or vents steam to release pressure.

<PIC>

To set the steam release valve, use the quick release button as follows:

1. For sealing / pressure cooking
Leave the quick release button popped up in the Seal position. When the pressure cooking lid is closed correctly, it automatically sets to Seal. Use this position for Pressure Cook or Steam programs so the cooker can build and maintain pressure.

2. For venting / quick release
Press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks into the Vent position. This opens the steam release valve and allows pressure to release quickly.

<PIC>

When the button is locked in the Vent position, hot steam will eject upward from the top of the steam release valve. This is normal during quick release. Once all pressure has been released, the float valve will drop down into the lid; only then is it safe to open the lid.

<PIC>

Important safety notes:

- Keep hands, face, and exposed skin away from the steam release valve while pressure is releasing.
- Do not cover, block, or place anything over the steam release valve.
- Do not try to open the lid while the float valve is raised.
- Do not force the lid open at any time.
- If you need to set the button back to Seal, use only a light flicking motion. Do not turn the quick release button more than 45 degrees; let it spring back to its original position.
- For foods that are thick, oily, high-starch, or expand during cooking, such as stews, chili, pasta, oatmeal, congee, beans, or grains, use Natural Release unless your recipe specifically says otherwise.,['Manual30_14', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02']

To set/install the anti-block shield on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, use the pressure cooking lid and follow these steps:

1. Turn the pressure cooking lid over so you can see the underside of the lid.
2. Locate the steam release pipe and the small prongs around it.
3. Position the anti-block shield directly over the prongs.
4. Press the shield down firmly until it snaps securely into place.

<PIC>

Before pressure cooking, always check that the anti-block shield is fitted properly and is not loose. Do not operate the pressure cooker without the anti-block shield installed.

The anti-block shield helps stop food particles from entering the steam release pipe. This supports proper pressure regulation and is required for safe pressure cooking.,['Manual30_35']

To install the float valve and silicone cap on the pressure cooking lid, please follow these steps:

1. From the top of the pressure cooking lid, drop the narrow end of the float valve into the float valve hole. The flat end of the float valve should remain on the top side of the lid.

<PIC>

2. Keep one finger on the flat top of the float valve so it does not fall out, then carefully turn the lid over.

3. On the underside of the lid, firmly push the silicone cap onto the bottom of the float valve until it is securely attached.

<PIC>

Before pressure cooking, always check that both the float valve and silicone cap are properly installed. These two parts work together to seal in pressurized steam. Do not operate the pressure cooker if either part is missing, loose, or incorrectly fitted.

For safety, never try to remove or force open the lid while the float valve is raised. The float valve must drop down and sit flush with the lid before you attempt to open it.,['Manual30_36', 'Manual30_37']

The condensation collector attaches to the back of the cooker base. Its job is to catch any liquid that overflows from the condensation rim during cooking, so it should be in place before you start cooking.

To install it:

1. Locate the tabs on the back of the cooker base.
2. Hold the condensation collector with its grooves facing those tabs.
3. Align the grooves on the collector over the tabs on the cooker base.
4. Slide the condensation collector straight into place until it sits securely.

<PIC>

After each use, remove the collector, empty any liquid, rinse it, and let it dry before reinstalling.

To remove it, pull the condensation collector straight away from the cooker base. Do not pull it downward, as this may make it harder to remove or affect the fit.,['Manual30_38']

To install the sealing ring for your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, use the pressure cooking lid and follow these steps:

1. Turn the pressure cooking lid over so you can see the underside of the lid.
2. Position the silicone sealing ring around the circular sealing ring rack.
3. Starting at one section, press the sealing ring firmly into place behind the rack.
4. Continue pressing all the way around the ring until it sits evenly and securely. Make sure there are no puckered, twisted, or loose sections.

<PIC>

When installed correctly, the sealing ring should sit snugly behind the sealing ring rack. A quick check is to turn the lid over: the sealing ring should stay in place and should not fall out.
