Before cooking, always inspect the sealing ring carefully. Only use an authorized sealing ring made for this multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer. Do not use the ring if it is stretched, cracked, cut, deformed, or damaged. The sealing ring can stretch with normal use, so it should be replaced every 12-18 months, or sooner if you notice wear, deformation, or looseness.,['Manual30_31']

To remove the silicone sealing ring from the pressure cooking lid, please follow these steps:

1. Make sure the cooker is unplugged, fully cooled to room temperature, and completely depressurized before handling the lid.

2. Turn the pressure cooking lid over so you can see the underside of the lid and the silicone sealing ring.

3. Grip the edge of the silicone sealing ring with your fingers, then gently pull it outward and away from behind the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack. Continue working around the lid until the entire ring comes free.

<PIC>

4. After removing the sealing ring, check the stainless-steel sealing ring rack. It should be centered, firmly secured, and the same height all the way around the lid. If the rack is bent, loose, uneven, or deformed, do not try to repair it yourself.

Tip: The sealing ring is usually easier to remove and reinstall when it is wet and soapy.

Important: Only use authorized sealing rings designed for this multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer. Do not use a sealing ring that is stretched, cut, deformed, cracked, or otherwise damaged. Before cooking, always confirm that the sealing ring is clean, undamaged, and correctly installed.,['Manual30_30']

### Sealing ring

The sealing ring is an important pressure-control part used with the pressure cooking lid. When the lid is closed, the sealing ring creates an airtight seal between the lid and the inner pot, allowing the cooker to build and maintain pressure properly.

Before pressure cooking, make sure the sealing ring is installed correctly. Only one sealing ring should be installed in the lid at a time.

<PIC>

Because the sealing ring is made of silicone, it can absorb strong aromas and certain flavors. To reduce flavor transfer between dishes, it is a good idea to keep extra sealing rings on hand, such as one for savory foods and one for sweet or mild foods.

### Important safety notes

Only use authorized sealing rings designed for this multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer. Do not use a sealing ring that is stretched, cracked, cut, deformed, or otherwise damaged.

Before each pressure cooking use, please check that:

- The sealing ring has no cuts, cracks, deformation, or damage.
- The sealing ring is fully seated behind the sealing ring rack.
- The sealing ring is not loose, puckered, or falling out.
- The lid and inner pot rim are clean and free from food residue.

Sealing rings naturally stretch over time with normal use. We recommend replacing the sealing ring every 12-18 months, or sooner if you notice stretching, deformation, damage, persistent odor, or steam leaking from the side of the lid.

If the sealing ring is missing, incorrectly installed, or damaged, food or steam may discharge during cooking, which may cause personal injury or property damage.

### Removing the silicone sealing ring

To remove it, grip the edge of the silicone sealing ring and gently pull it out from behind the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack on the underside of the pressure cooking lid.

After removing the sealing ring, check the stainless-steel rack. It should be centered, secure, and even in height all the way around the lid. If the rack appears bent or deformed, do not try to repair it yourself.

<PIC>

### Installing the silicone sealing ring

Place the sealing ring over the sealing ring rack, then press it firmly into place all the way around. Make sure it sits smoothly behind the rack without puckering or folding.

When installed correctly, the sealing ring should fit snugly behind the sealing ring rack and should not fall out when the lid is turned over.

<PIC>

Tip: The sealing ring is usually easier to remove and install when it is wet and soapy. After cleaning or reinstalling, allow all parts to dry thoroughly before use or storage.,['Manual30_16', 'Manual30_30', 'Manual30_31']

The steam release valve is a key pressure-control and safety part on the pressure cooking lid of the multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer. It sits on top of the steam release pipe and is required whenever you use pressure cooking functions.

When the cooker releases pressure, hot steam comes out through the top of the steam release valve. The valve is designed to sit loosely on the steam release pipe, so a little movement is normal, but it must be installed before cooking.

<PIC>

Important safety reminder: do not cover, block, or obstruct the steam release valve in any way. Keep hands, face, and exposed skin away from the valve during operation and while there may still be residual pressure inside the cooker.

The quick release button controls the steam release valve. To perform a Quick Release, press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks into the Vent position.

<PIC>

Once the button is in the Vent position, a strong jet of steam will eject from the top of the steam release valve. This is expected during pressure release. Wait until the float valve drops before opening the lid.

<PIC>

Please also note these usage and safety points:
- Do not lean over the steam release valve while the appliance is operating or depressurizing.
- Do not place hands, face, towels, cloths, or any object over the steam release valve.
- Before pressure cooking, check that the steam release valve, steam release pipe, anti-block shield, and float valve are clean and not clogged.
- The steam release valve must be installed before using the cooker.
- If steam escapes from the steam release valve in a steady stream for longer than about 3 minutes during cooking, turn the appliance off and allow it to depressurize safely before checking the parts.

To remove the steam release valve for cleaning or inspection, pull it straight up and off the steam release pipe.

<PIC>

To reinstall it, place the steam release valve back onto the steam release pipe and press down firmly. When installed correctly, it will still sit loosely, but it should stay in place when the lid is turned over.

<PIC>,['Manual30_15', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02', 'Manual30_32', 'Manual30_33']

The quick release button is part of the pressure cooking lid. It controls the steam release valve, which determines whether the cooker stays sealed to build/hold pressure or vents steam to release pressure.

<PIC>

There are two key positions:

- Seal position: the button is popped up. This keeps the steam release valve closed so the cooker can pressurize.
- Vent position: the button is pressed down and locked. This opens the steam release valve so pressure can be released.

What makes it convenient is that you can release pressure without putting your hand directly over the steam. The button lets you operate the valve from a safer distance.

One thing to note: although the lid marking says “Turn to Seal,” the action is more like a small flick. Do not rotate the quick release button more than about 45 degrees. After flicking it to Seal, let it spring back to its original raised position.

For Quick Release, also called QR or QPR, press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks into the Vent position.

<PIC>

Once the button is locked in Vent, hot steam will eject upward through the top of the steam release valve. This is normal. Keep your hands, face, and any exposed skin away from the steam release area while pressure is releasing.

<PIC>

When all pressure has been released, the float valve will drop down into the lid. Only after the float valve has dropped should you open the pressure cooking lid. Never force the lid open while the float valve is still raised.

Important safety notes:

- Do not cover, block, or place anything over the steam release valve.
- Do not lean over the steam release valve or float valve during use or while pressure remains inside.
- Do not use Quick Release for fatty, oily, thick, or high-starch foods such as stews, chili, pasta, oatmeal, congee, or similar dishes, as contents may splatter.
- Do not use Quick Release for foods that expand during cooking, such as beans and grains. For these foods, allow pressure to release naturally.,['Manual30_14', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02']

Natural Release, also called NR or NPR, is a pressure-release method where cooking stops gradually and the cooker depressurizes on its own as the temperature inside drops. No steam is intentionally vented by pressing the quick release button during this process.

<PIC>

How to use Natural Release:
1. When the cooking program finishes, leave the quick release button in the popped-up Seal position.
2. Turn Keep Warm off, unless your recipe specifically says otherwise.
3. Wait until the cooker releases pressure naturally.
4. Open the lid only after the float valve has dropped down into the lid.

What to expect:
The food may continue to cook while the pressure is coming down. This is normal and is often called carryover cooking or resting. The release time can vary depending on the amount and type of food inside, and it may take up to 40 minutes.

When to choose NR:
Natural Release is recommended for high-starch foods such as soups, stews, chilis, pasta, oatmeal, and congee. It is also best for foods that expand during cooking, such as beans and grains, because it helps reduce the chance of splattering or foaming through the steam release valve.

Important safety reminder:
Do not try to remove the lid while the float valve is still raised. The cooker is still pressurized at that point. Never force the lid open; wait until the float valve has fully dropped before opening.,['Manual30_9']

The robot anatomy of the vacuum can be understood in three main areas: the top body parts, the control buttons and indicators, and the underside cleaning components. Top View From the top, the main visible parts are: - iAdapt® Localization Camera: Helps the robot recognize its position and navigate during cleaning. - Handle: Used for lifting or carrying the vacuum when needed. - Bin Release Button: Press this button to remove the dust bin for emptying or maintenance. Buttons and Indicators The control panel includes three main buttons: - DOCK Button: Sends the vacuum back to its charging base. - CLEAN Button: Wakes the vacuum, starts cleaning, pauses cleaning, or resumes cleaning. - SPOT Button: Starts spot cleaning for a concentrated area of debris. The status indicators above the buttons help show the vacuum’s current condition: - Wi-Fi® Indicator: Shows Wi-Fi connection status. - Troubleshooting Indicator: Lights when the vacuum needs attention or has an error. - Battery Indicator: Shows battery or charging status. - Full Bin Indicator: Alerts you when the dust bin needs to be emptied. - Dirt Detect™ Indicator: Lights when the vacuum detects a higher concentration of dirt and cleans that area more thoroughly. Bottom View The underside contains the main cleaning and floor-contact components: - Debris Extractors: The dual rollers that lift and pull debris from the floor. - Extractor Caps: End caps on the extractors that may need to be removed for cleaning hair or debris. - Extractor Frame: Holds the extractors in place. - Extractor Frame Release Tabs: Used to open the extractor frame for cleaning or maintenance. - Dust Bin: Collects dirt and debris picked up during cleaning. - Side Brush: Sweeps dust and debris from edges and corners into the cleaning path. - Charging Contacts: Connect with the charging base so the vacuum can recharge. - Floor Tracking Sensor: Helps the vacuum track movement across the floor for navigation.,['Manual32_1', 'Manual32_2', 'Manual32_3']

The two primary modes are Halo Mode and Virtual Wall Mode. These modes are set on the Dual Mode Virtual Wall Barrier and help you control where the vacuum should clean and which areas it should avoid. Halo Mode: Move the switch to the down position to create a circular protective zone around the device. The vacuum will not enter this invisible boundary, which helps protect items such as pet bowls, vases, or other objects you do not want the vacuum to bump into. The protected area reaches approximately 24 inches from the center of the device. Virtual Wall Mode: Move the switch to the up position to create an invisible wall that blocks the vacuum from crossing into certain areas. This is useful for doorways, room openings, or spaces you want to keep off-limits. In this mode, the device can block openings of up to 10 feet with an invisible cone-shaped barrier. In short, use Halo Mode to protect a specific object or small area, and use Virtual Wall Mode to block a larger opening or prevent the vacuum from entering another room.,['Manual32_4', 'Manual32_5', 'Manual32_6']

To empty the vacuum cleaner's bin, please follow these steps:

1. Press the bin release button to remove the dust bin from the vacuum. The release button is located on the bin area, as shown below.

<PIC>

2. Hold the bin over a trash container, then open the bin door and empty out the debris.

<PIC>

After emptying, close the bin door securely and reinstall the bin into the vacuum before continuing use.

For best performance, empty the bin after each cleaning session and clean the filter regularly. If the full bin indicator turns on during a cleaning job, you can pause the vacuum, empty the bin, reinstall it, and then resume cleaning. If the full bin indicator stays on even when the bin does not look full, the full bin sensors may need to be cleaned.,['Manual32_8', 'Manual32_9']

To clean the vacuum cleaner filter, please follow these steps:

1. Press the bin release button and remove the dust bin from the vacuum.
2. Open the bin door and empty the dust bin into a trash container.
3. Remove the filter by holding the yellow tab and pulling the filter out.

<PIC>

4. Hold the filter over a trash container, then gently tap it against the inside of the container to shake off loose dust and debris. Do not rinse or wash the filter with water.

<PIC>

5. Reinsert the filter into the bin with the yellow tab facing up.
6. Close the filter door, then reinstall the bin securely into the vacuum.

Important: The filter door will not close unless the filter is correctly reinserted. After each use, empty the bin and clean the filter. For regular maintenance, clean the filter once per week, or twice per week if you have pets, and replace the filter every 2 months.,['Manual32_10', 'Manual32_11']

To clean the full bin sensors properly, please follow these steps:

1. Remove the dust bin from the vacuum and empty it completely. Press the bin release button, pull out the bin, and make sure any debris inside has been discarded before cleaning the sensors.

<PIC>

2. With the bin removed, locate the full bin sensors inside the vacuum’s bin compartment. Wipe the sensors gently using a clean, dry cloth. Do not use water, sprays, or cleaning solution on the sensors.

<PIC>

3. Next, clean the sensor ports on the dust bin itself. Wipe both the inner sensor ports inside the bin door and the outer sensor ports on the outside of the bin with a clean, dry cloth.

<PIC>

Full bin sensors should be cleaned once every 2 weeks. Also clean them any time the full bin indicator is on but the bin does not actually appear to be full, as dust on the sensors or sensor ports may cause a false full-bin alert.,['Manual32_12', 'Manual32_13', 'Manual32_14']

To clean the extractors, please turn the vacuum over and follow these steps:

1. Open the extractor frame.
Pinch the yellow extractor frame release tabs, then lift the extractor frame upward. Check the opening and remove any visible obstructions, such as clumps of dust, hair, or larger debris.

<PIC>

2. Clean under the extractor caps and around the axles.
Remove both extractors from the cleaning head. Pull off the yellow extractor caps, then clear away any hair, thread, or debris wrapped around the metal axles or collected underneath the caps. After cleaning, reinstall the extractor caps securely.

<PIC>

3. Clean the opposite ends of the extractors.
Check the square and hexagonal plastic pegs on the other side of each extractor. Remove any hair or debris wrapped around these peg ends so the extractors can rotate smoothly.

<PIC>

4. Clear the vacuum path.
With the extractors removed, inspect the open vacuum path inside the cleaning head and remove any debris blocking the airflow or the extractor compartment.

<PIC>

5. Reinstall the extractors correctly.
Place the extractors back into the cleaning head, making sure the color and shape of each extractor peg match the corresponding color and shape icons on the cleaning head module. Once aligned, close the extractor frame until it is seated properly.

Maintenance tip: Clean the extractors once every 4 months, or once every 3 months if you have pets. For best cleaning performance, replace the extractors every 6-12 months.,['Manual32_19', 'Manual32_20', 'Manual32_21', 'Manual32_22']

To clean the sensors and charging contacts properly, please follow these steps:

1. Before cleaning, disconnect the vacuum from the charger or Home Base to ensure safe maintenance. Use only a clean, dry cloth, and do not spray cleaning solution directly onto any sensors, sensor openings, or electronic areas.

2. Turn the vacuum over and locate the sensors on the underside of the unit. Gently wipe each sensor surface with the clean, dry cloth. Pay special attention to the sensors around the outer edge and the floor tracking sensor area, as dust buildup here may affect navigation and floor detection.

<PIC>

3. Next, clean the charging contacts on the vacuum. These are the metal contact points on the underside/front area of the robot. Wipe them carefully with the same type of clean, dry cloth to remove dust, film, or debris.

4. Also wipe the charging contacts on the Home Base. Cleaning both the vacuum contacts and the Home Base contacts helps ensure the vacuum can dock and charge reliably.

<PIC>

For best performance, clean the charging contacts and related sensors about once per month, or more often if the vacuum is used in a dusty environment.,['Manual32_17', 'Manual32_18']

To position the vacuum cleaner’s Home Base correctly, place it in an open, uncluttered indoor area where the vacuum has enough space to leave, return, and dock smoothly.

Please keep the following clearance around the Home Base:

1. Leave at least 1.5 feet of open space on each side.
2. Leave at least 4 feet of open space in front of the Home Base.
3. Keep the Home Base at least 4 feet away from stairs or drop-off areas.
4. Keep it at least 8 feet away from any virtual wall barriers.

Also, keep the Home Base plugged in at all times so the vacuum can stay charged and ready to clean. For best app performance, place it in an area with consistent Wi-Fi coverage so the vacuum can reliably receive updates, schedules, and commands from the app.

To clean the front caster wheel, follow these steps:

1. Pull firmly upward on the front caster wheel to remove the wheel assembly from its housing. The image below shows the wheel, axle, and housing, as well as the direction to pull for removal.

<PIC>

2. Check the wheel cavity and remove any dust, hair, string, or other debris from inside the opening.

3. Spin the wheel by hand to confirm it turns freely. If the wheel feels stuck, tight, or does not rotate smoothly, remove the wheel from the housing and push firmly to take out the axle. Clear away any hair or debris wrapped around the axle or inside the wheel.

4. Reassemble the wheel, axle, and housing after cleaning. Push the front caster wheel back into place until it clicks securely.

For best performance, clean the front caster wheel once per month, or more often if hair or debris builds up around the wheel.,['Manual32_15']

To clean the side brush, please follow these steps:

1. Turn the vacuum over so you can access the underside and locate the side brush.

2. Use a coin or a small screwdriver to loosen and remove the screw in the center of the side brush, as shown below.

<PIC>

3. Lift the side brush off the brush post.

4. Remove any hair, dust, or debris from both the side brush and the brush post. Make sure the brush post area is clear so the brush can spin freely after reinstallation.

5. Place the side brush back onto the brush post, then reinstall and tighten the screw securely. Do not overtighten it.

Care tip: Clean the side brush about once per month to help maintain good cleaning performance.,['Manual32_16']

The dual-mode virtual wall barrier is used to guide where the vacuum can and cannot clean. It creates an invisible boundary that the vacuum can detect, helping keep it inside the areas you want cleaned and away from areas or objects you want to protect. You can choose between two modes depending on your cleaning needs.

<PIC>

Halo Mode: Move the switch to the down position to create a circular protective zone around the device. The vacuum will not enter this zone, so this mode is useful for protecting items such as pet bowls, vases, or a specific corner you do not want the vacuum to approach. The halo is invisible and extends approximately 24 inches from the center of the device, creating about a 4-foot protected area across.

<PIC>

Virtual Wall Mode: Move the switch to the up position to create an invisible wall that blocks the vacuum from crossing into another area. This is helpful for doorways, room entrances, or open spaces you want to keep the vacuum out of. In this mode, the barrier can block openings up to 10 feet, and the invisible barrier becomes wider as it extends farther from the device.

<PIC>

When not using the barrier for an extended period, switch it to the middle Off position to help preserve battery life. Under normal use, the batteries usually last about 8 to 10 months.,['Manual32_4', 'Manual32_5', 'Manual32_6']

If the vacuum detects a problem, it will alert you in three ways: it will play a two-tone distress sound, give a spoken message explaining the issue, and the troubleshooting indicator will blink. You can use the indicator panel shown below to identify the troubleshooting indicator and the CLEAN button used for rebooting.

<PIC>

Please try the following troubleshooting steps:

1. Listen to the spoken message from the vacuum. This message usually explains what needs attention, such as a full bin, a blockage, or a sensor-related issue.

2. Check the blinking troubleshooting indicator. When this indicator flashes, it confirms that the vacuum has detected an error or needs assistance.

3. If the issue does not clear after checking the obvious causes, reboot the vacuum. Press and hold the CLEAN button for 10 seconds until all indicators illuminate, then release the button. After releasing it, you should hear a tone confirming that the reboot was successful.

4. After the reboot, try starting the vacuum again to see whether the problem has been resolved.

5. If you use scheduled cleaning, open the app after rebooting and confirm that your schedule is still set correctly.

For additional help, the app and online support resources can provide more detailed guidance and videos for specific error messages.,['Manual32_2']

To mount the camera or equipment on a drop ceiling T-rail, use the included drop ceiling mounting hardware. The hardware is designed to fit most 9/16", 15/16", or 1 1/2" T-rails.

1. Set the T-rail clip spacing

Use the dashed guide lines on the mount plate template to determine the correct spacing between the T-rail clips. Align the clips with the guide lines so they will match the mounting holes used for T-rail installation.

<PIC>

2. Secure the clips

Once the clips are positioned correctly, tighten the set screws on the T-rail clips. Use the included 5/64" (2 mm) hex key to secure them firmly.

<PIC>

3. Attach the mount plate to the T-rail clips

Fasten the mount plate to the T-rail clips using the mount plate holes marked with “G”. Make sure the screws pass through the correct “G” holes and into the center of each T-rail clip.

<PIC>

4. Snap the clips onto the T-rail

Attach the T-rail clips to the ceiling T-rail by rotating them into position and snapping them onto the rail as shown. After installation, the black foam pads should be slightly compressed, which helps keep the assembly stable against the T-rail.

<PIC>

5. Choose the correct screws for your T-rail type

For a standard T-rail installation, use the standard 6-32 x 4 mm T-rail screws.

For a recessed T-rail installation, use the rubber spacers together with the longer 6-32 x 7 mm screws. The rubber spacer should sit between the mount plate and the T-rail clip to compensate for the recessed rail position.

<PIC>

After the mount plate is securely installed on the T-rail, continue with connecting the PoE cable and attaching the camera to the mount plate according to the normal camera mounting steps.,['Manual33_10', 'Manual33_11', 'Manual33_12', 'Manual33_13', 'Manual33_14']

To power the camera, turn the power switch to an operating position. The camera will only work after this switch is turned on.

<PIC>

Power switch positions:

<OFF>: The camera is turned off and will not operate.

<ON>: The camera is turned on and ready for normal operation.

</>: The camera is turned on, and the camera controls can be operated.

To turn the camera on, rotate the switch from <OFF> to either <ON> or </>, depending on how you want to use the camera.

<PIC>

To save battery power, the camera will turn off automatically after about 1 minute of no operation. If this happens, the camera is not fully switched off; you can wake it up again by pressing the shutter button.

You can also change how long the camera waits before turning off automatically by adjusting the menu setting for [Auto power off].,['Manual10_29', 'Manual10_30']

The brake lever is used to slow down or stop the machine by braking the entire drive system. To apply the brake, first release the throttle, then squeeze the brake lever smoothly toward the handlebar. When the brake is applied, the brake light will illuminate.

<PIC>

For normal slowing or stopping, apply the brake gently and progressively. Avoid sudden or excessive braking, especially on ice, hard-packed snow, or downhill sections, because the drive track may lose traction or lock, reducing control and increasing the risk of an accident.

The parking brake button is used when parking the machine or before starting the engine. To set the parking brake:
1. Squeeze the brake lever toward the handlebar.
2. While holding the lever, push down the parking brake button.
3. Release the brake lever so the parking brake stays engaged.

To release the parking brake, simply squeeze the brake lever again.

<PIC>

Important safety reminders:
- Always set the parking brake before attempting to start the engine.
- Never operate or ride the machine with the parking brake applied, as this can overheat the brake disc and reduce braking performance.
- Before riding, check that the brake operates properly at low speed.
- Make sure the brake lever end does not extend beyond the handlebar end, which helps prevent lever damage if the machine is placed on its side for service.,['Manual34_39', 'Manual34_40']

Before using a snowmobile, complete the following preparation checks to make sure the machine is safe, properly supplied, and ready to operate:

1. Review all safety labels and become familiar with the controls

Before starting, check the warning and instruction labels on the snowmobile. Make sure you understand the function of the throttle, brake, steering, engine stop switch, parking brake, and other operating controls. If any control or function is unclear, do not ride until it has been confirmed.

<PIC>

2. Check throttle, steering, and brake operation

Always perform the pre-operation checks before starting the engine. Confirm that:
- The throttle lever moves smoothly.
- The throttle returns fully to the idle position when released.
- The steering moves correctly without abnormal looseness or binding.
- The brake operates properly and provides normal stopping resistance.

<PIC>

3. Apply the parking brake before starting

Set the parking brake before attempting to start the engine. After the engine is started and you are ready to move, release the parking brake. Do not ride with the parking brake applied, as this can overheat the brake disc and reduce braking performance.

4. Check the fuel level and fuel cap

Make sure there is enough fuel in the tank for your ride. After refueling, tighten the fuel tank cap securely. Keep snow and ice out of the fuel tank, refuel outdoors, and wipe up any spilled fuel immediately.

<PIC>

5. Check the engine oil level

Confirm that there is sufficient oil in the oil tank before riding. The oil level should be within the indicated range. If it is low, refill with the recommended 2-cycle oil before operating the snowmobile.

<PIC>

6. Inspect the manual starter

Check the manual starter handle and starter rope before use. The rope should pull smoothly and should not show signs of fraying, damage, or breakage.

<PIC>

7. Inspect the main machine components

Before riding, also check the condition of the drive track, slide runners, skis and ski runners, steering system, lights, battery, fittings, fasteners, service tools, and spare parts. For longer trips, it is good practice to carry extra fuel, oil, a drive belt, bulbs, spark plugs, and basic repair items.

<PIC>

8. Wear proper protective clothing

Wear an approved helmet, face shield or goggles, warm snowmobile clothing, boots, and gloves or mittens that still allow full use of your thumbs and fingers for operating the controls.

<PIC>

Important safety reminders: never operate the snowmobile after drinking alcohol or taking drugs, do not allow anyone to stand behind the machine while starting or inspecting it, and handle fuel carefully because it is highly flammable. Also, do not operate the snowmobile on public roads, do not carry passengers if the machine is designed for the operator only, and avoid riding on surfaces other than snow or ice.,['Manual34_7', 'Manual34_97', 'Manual34_93', 'Manual34_96', 'Manual34_98', 'Manual34_108', 'Manual34_11']

Before starting the snowmobile engine, please complete the basic safety checks first: make sure the parking brake is applied, the throttle, brake, and steering operate normally, and no one is standing behind the machine. Also confirm that the engine stop switch is in the “ON” position.

1. Set the starter lever, also called the choke, according to engine temperature.

For a cold engine, move the starter lever to the fully open position. For warming up, use the half-open position where applicable. For warm engine starting, return the starter lever to the close position.

The starter lever positions are:
① Starter lever/choke
② Fully open: cold engine starting
③ Half-open: engine warm-up, applicable to CS340E/ET410TR/VK540E
④ Close: warm engine starting

<PIC>

2. Start the engine according to your snowmobile’s starting type.

For a manual starting model:
Turn the main switch to the “ON” position. Then pull the recoil starter slowly until you feel it engage, and after that pull it briskly to start the engine.

Once the engine starts, move the starter lever/choke to the “Half-open” position. Let the engine warm up until it runs smoothly and does not run roughly or begin to stall when the starter lever is returned to the “Close” position.

① “ON”

<PIC>

For an electric starting model:
Turn the main switch to the “START” position. After the engine starts, release the switch immediately, then move the starter lever/choke to the “Half-open” position. Warm up the engine until it runs smoothly and does not run roughly or begin to stall when the starter lever is returned to the “Close” position.

① “START”

<PIC>

Important caution for electric starting: release the switch as soon as the engine starts. If the engine does not start, release the switch, wait a few seconds, and try again. Keep each starting attempt as short as possible to protect the battery, and do not crank the engine for more than 10 seconds in any single attempt.

Safety reminders: keep the parking brake applied while starting, but never ride with the parking brake applied. Do not allow anyone to stand behind the snowmobile during starting, inspection, or adjustment. Do not run the engine indoors except briefly when moving the machine into or out of a building, and open outside doors because exhaust fumes are dangerous.,['Manual34_24', 'Manual34_116', 'Manual34_117']

A V-Belt Holder is a designated storage holder used to secure a spare V-belt on the machine for emergency use. Keeping a spare V-belt in this holder helps ensure that a replacement belt is available if the installed V-belt becomes worn, damaged, or needs replacement during operation.

To remove the spare V-belt from the holder, loosen the bolt marked ①, then release the belt from the holder.

<PIC>

When placing a spare V-belt back into the holder, make sure it sits correctly in the holder and then tighten the bolt securely. The belt should not be loose or able to shift during operation.

<PIC>

Important cautions:

- Always tighten the bolt after installing the V-belt in the holder.
- Make sure the V-belt is firmly secured in the holder.
- Confirm that the belt does not come loose, because it may be damaged if it contacts the hot muffler.

<PIC>,['Manual34_51', 'Manual34_52', 'Manual34_55']

Before adjusting the throttle cable, make sure the engine idle speed has already been adjusted correctly. This is important because throttle cable free play should be set only after the idle setting is correct.

Follow these steps:

1. Loosen the throttle cable adjuster locknut.

2. Turn the adjuster in or out until the throttle lever free play is within the specified range. The locknut and adjuster are shown in the illustration: ② is the locknut and ③ is the adjuster.

<PIC>

Throttle lever free play specifications:

BR250T, ET410TR, VK540E: 1.0-2.0 mm (0.04-0.08 in)

CS340E: 0.5-1.0 mm (0.02-0.04 in)

3. After the correct free play is obtained, tighten the locknut securely so the adjustment does not change.

<PIC>

<PIC>

After adjustment, operate the throttle lever several times and confirm that it moves smoothly and returns properly. Do not ride the snowmobile if the throttle does not move freely or return correctly.,['Manual34_148', 'Manual34_149', 'Manual34_150']

Before starting the engine, always confirm that the throttle, brake, and steering operate properly. The steering check should include making sure the handlebars and skis move smoothly and that there is no excessive looseness or unusual play in the steering system.

<PIC>

When riding, steer by turning the handlebar in the direction you want to go. On most snow surfaces, proper turning also depends on your body position. As you approach a curve, slow down first, then begin turning the handlebars in the desired direction. At the same time, shift your weight onto the running board on the inside of the turn and lean your upper body into the turn. This body-weight movement helps the snowmobile stay balanced and respond more predictably.

<PIC>

Practice this turning technique at low speed in a large, flat, open area with no obstacles or other traffic. Repeat it many times before attempting tighter turns or higher speeds. As the turn becomes sharper, or as speed increases, you will need to lean farther into the turn.

Avoid abrupt throttle changes, excessive braking, incorrect body movement, or entering a turn too fast for its sharpness. These actions can reduce control and may cause the snowmobile to tip. If the snowmobile begins to tip while turning, lean farther into the turn to help regain balance. If needed, gradually release the throttle and/or steer toward the outside of the turn.

On ice or icy surfaces, use extra caution. Drive slowly, avoid rapid acceleration, sharp turning, and hard braking. Steering response is greatly reduced on ice, and uncontrolled spins can occur easily.,['Manual34_108', 'Manual34_127']

For most snow surfaces, the key to turning a snowmobile is proper body positioning, often called “body English.” Because a snowmobile responds strongly to rider weight and balance, turning should be done smoothly and with your body leaning into the turn.

Follow these steps when approaching and making a turn:

1. Slow down before the curve. Reduce speed early so you enter the turn under control.

2. Begin turning the handlebars in the direction you want to go.

3. At the same time, shift your weight to the running board on the inside of the turn.

4. Lean your upper body into the turn to help keep the snowmobile balanced and tracking properly.

<PIC>

Practice this technique many times at slow speed in a large, flat, open area with no obstacles. Once you are comfortable, you can gradually apply the same method at higher speeds or in tighter curves. The sharper the turn or the higher the speed, the more you will need to lean into the turn.

Be careful to avoid sudden throttle changes, excessive braking, incorrect body movement, or entering the turn too fast. Any of these can cause the snowmobile to tip.

If the snowmobile begins to tip while turning, lean farther into the turn to regain balance. If needed, gradually ease off the throttle and/or steer toward the outside of the turn.

Remember: avoid higher speeds until you are thoroughly familiar with how your snowmobile handles and responds.,['Manual34_127']

When riding uphill on a snowmobile, start with gentle slopes first. Only attempt steeper or more difficult climbs after you have built enough confidence, balance, and throttle control.

<PIC>

As you approach the hill, build momentum before the climb begins. Once the snowmobile starts climbing, reduce the throttle opening slightly to help prevent the drive track from spinning or slipping. Smooth throttle control is important: too little power may cause you to lose momentum, while too much throttle can reduce traction.

Keep your body weight on the uphill side at all times. For a straight uphill climb, lean forward to keep the front of the snowmobile stable and maintain control. On steeper inclines, stand on the running boards and lean forward over the handlebars so your weight stays properly positioned.

As you near the crest of the hill, slow down and stay alert. There may be obstacles, sharp drops, people, or other vehicles on the other side, so be ready to react immediately.

If you cannot continue climbing, do not spin the track. Stop the engine, set the parking brake, and then pull the rear of the snowmobile around so the machine points back downhill. Stay on the uphill side of the snowmobile while doing this; do not move to the downhill side. Once the snowmobile is facing downhill, restart the engine, release the parking brake, and descend carefully.

WARNING: Side hills and steep slopes are not recommended for beginner or novice snowmobile riders.,['Manual34_127']

When riding downhill on a snowmobile, the key is to stay slow, controlled, and balanced throughout the descent.

1. Keep your speed to a minimum before and during the downhill section. Do not allow the snowmobile to coast freely down the hill.

2. Apply just enough throttle to keep the clutch engaged. This helps the engine compression slow the snowmobile naturally and gives you better control than simply rolling downhill.

3. Use the brake lightly and frequently. Apply gentle pressure in short, controlled applications rather than braking hard or suddenly.

<PIC>

4. Maintain a steady riding posture and stay alert to changes in the snow surface, slope angle, or hidden obstacles. Plan your descent early so you do not need sudden steering or heavy braking.

WARNING: Use extra caution when braking while descending. Excessive braking can lock the track, which may cause the snowmobile to lose traction and result in loss of control.,['Manual34_128']

Crossing the face of a slope, also called side-hilling, requires careful body positioning and smooth control so the snowmobile stays balanced across the incline.

Important: side hills and steep slopes are not recommended for beginner or novice riders. Practice on gentle terrain first and only attempt this technique when you are comfortable controlling the snowmobile at low speeds.

When riding across a slope, keep your body weight shifted toward the uphill side. A recommended position is to kneel with the knee of your downhill leg on the seat, while keeping the foot of your uphill leg on the uphill running board. This stance helps you move your weight quickly if the snowmobile begins to slide or tip.

<PIC>

Because snow and ice can be slippery, be prepared for the snowmobile to slide sideways while crossing the slope. If it begins to slide and there are no obstacles in your path, steer in the direction of the slide. As balance and traction return, gradually steer back toward your intended direction of travel.

If the snowmobile starts to tip, steer down the hill to help regain balance.

Warning: if you cannot maintain proper balance and the snowmobile is going to tip over, get off immediately on the uphill side. Do not dismount on the downhill side.,['Manual34_129']

To inspect the spark plug on your snowmobile, follow these steps carefully:

1. Remove and inspect the spark plug periodically. Heat, carbon deposits, and normal use can gradually cause the spark plug to break down, erode, or become fouled.

2. Check the color of the white porcelain insulator around the center electrode. For a machine being ridden under normal conditions, the ideal color is medium to light tan.

3. Watch for abnormal spark plug color. If the porcelain around the center electrode is a distinctly different color, it may indicate an engine condition that needs attention. For example, a very white porcelain color may point to an intake air leak or carburetion issue for that cylinder. In this case, do not try to diagnose or adjust it yourself; have the snowmobile checked by a dealer.

4. Before reinstalling or installing a spark plug, measure the electrode gap with a wire thickness gauge and adjust it to the specified value. In the illustration below, item 1 shows the spark plug reach, and item 2 shows the electrode gap.

<PIC>

5. Confirm that the spark plug has the correct reach, which is the thread length from the gasket seat to the end of the threaded portion. The correct reach depends on the model:

- BR250T: 12.7 mm (0.50 in)
- CS340E / ET410TR / VK540E: 19.0 mm (0.75 in)

Using the wrong reach can cause problems. If the reach is too long, overheating and engine damage may occur. If it is too short, the spark plug may foul, performance may drop, and carbon can build up on exposed threads.

<PIC>

6. When installing the spark plug, clean the gasket surface first. Wipe any grime or deposits from the threads, then tighten the spark plug to the proper torque.

Spark plug tightening torque: 28 Nm (2.8 m-kg, 20 ft-lb)

Use the correct spark plug type for your model. If you are considering changing to a different type of spark plug, check with a dealer first.,['Manual34_139', 'Manual34_138']

To clean a snowmobile properly, follow these steps:

1. Clean the snowmobile thoroughly, both inside and outside, to remove corrosive salts, acids, mud, grease, and other buildup.

2. Apply Mud and Grease Release, or an equivalent cleaner, to areas with stuck-on mud, grease, or grime. Let it loosen the buildup before washing.

3. Wash the machine with mild soap. Avoid harsh cleaners that may damage painted, plastic, or metal surfaces.

4. Rinse the snowmobile completely with clean water, making sure no soap or loosened debris remains.

5. Dry the machine completely after rinsing. Pay extra attention to metal parts, suspension areas, and hidden spots where moisture can remain and cause corrosion.

Important: If the snowmobile was transported in an open trailer or truck through areas where road salt is used, clean it as soon as you arrive at your destination. Road salt can quickly cause corrosion if left on the machine.

You can memorize TV channels manually in two ways: by using the MEMORY/ERASE button for quick changes, or by using the MENU for step-by-step channel setup. Manual programming is especially useful after Auto Program has already saved available channels and you only need to add or remove specific channels.

**Method 1: Using the MEMORY/ERASE button**

1. Use the ▲/▼ buttons to move to the channel you want, or enter the channel directly with the NUMBER buttons.
2. Press the MEMORY/ERASE button.
3. Each press lets you choose whether that channel is stored in memory or erased from memory.

<PIC>

When a channel is set to **Memory**, it can be selected later using the channel up/down buttons. When it is set to **Erase**, the TV skips it during normal channel browsing, although you may still be able to enter it directly with the number buttons.

**Method 2: Using the MENU**

1. Press the MENU button.
2. Use the ▲/▼ buttons until the **CH.** channel menu appears.
3. Press ▶, then use ▲/▼ to select **Manual**.
4. Press ▶ again, then use ▲/▼ to select **Channel**.
5. Use the adjustment button and ▲/▼ to choose the channel number you want to memorize or erase.

<PIC>

6. Move to **Memory** in the Manual menu.
7. Press ▶, then use ▲/▼ to choose:
   - **On**: memorizes the selected channel.
   - **Off**: removes the selected channel from memory.

<PIC>

8. Press the MENU button repeatedly to exit when you are finished.

For best results, run Auto Program first so the TV stores the active channels in your area, then use Manual Program to add any missed channels or remove channels you do not want to browse through.,['Manual35_9', 'Manual35_10', 'Manual35_11']

Poor television or radio reception is usually related to signal interference, weak signal strength, antenna position, or connection issues. The most common causes and conditions include the following:

1. Ignition or electrical interference

This type of interference can make the picture flutter, drift, show black spots, or display horizontal streaks. It is commonly caused by nearby automobile ignition systems, neon lamps, electrical drills, or other electrical appliances operating close to the TV or antenna line.

<PIC>

2. Ghosting or double images

Ghosting happens when the broadcast signal reaches the TV through more than one path. One signal travels directly to the antenna, while another is reflected by tall buildings, hills, or other large objects. This can create a second, shadow-like image on the screen. Adjusting the antenna direction or moving the antenna to a better position may help improve reception.

<PIC>

3. Snow or speckled picture

A snowy picture usually means the receiver is in a weak or fringe signal area. The image may be covered with small dots because the signal arriving at the TV is not strong enough. In this situation, using a better-positioned antenna or installing a special outdoor antenna may improve the picture quality.

<PIC>

Other conditions that may also cause poor reception include:

- Strong random interference from cars, airplanes, or nearby electrical sources.
- A weak antenna signal.
- Severely weathered outdoor antennas or antenna cables.
- Loose, damaged, or poor antenna connections.
- Local interference near the TV or antenna.
- Broadcast station issues, which can sometimes affect only one channel.

To improve reception, please check that the antenna or cable connection is secure, try another channel to confirm whether the issue is station-related, adjust the antenna direction or position, and inspect any outdoor antenna or cable for weathering or damage. If the picture remains unstable, running channel programming again may also help the TV store the clearest available channels.,['television0_01', 'television0_02', 'television0_03']

Closed captioning turns the audio portion of a TV program into written words shown on the screen, similar to subtitles. This can help you follow dialogue and narration, especially when the audio is difficult to hear or when captions are preferred.

<PIC>

You can turn captions or text services on in either of the following ways:

Using the CAPTION button:
1. Press the CAPTION button on the remote control.
2. Press the CAPTION button repeatedly to choose the setting you want: OFF, Mode1, Mode2, Text1, or Text2.
3. Press the ENTER(O) button to confirm and exit.

<PIC>

Using the MENU button:
1. Press the MENU button.
2. Use the up/down buttons to select the Special menu.
3. Press the right button, then use the up/down buttons to select Caption.
4. Press the right button, then use the up/down buttons to select Off, Mode1, Mode2, Text1, or Text2.
5. Press the MENU button repeatedly to exit.

<PIC>

Option details:
- Off: Turns captions/text off.
- Mode1: Usually displays the main closed caption signal for the program.
- Mode2: May display a second caption signal, such as demonstration or other program information, if provided by the broadcaster.
- Text1/Text2: Displays text-service information, such as weather, news, program lists, or other station-provided information, when available.

Please note:
- Not all TV broadcasts include closed caption signals.
- Some stations may provide captions but not text services.
- If the TV signal is weak or there is interference, captions may not appear correctly, or a blank black box may appear in text mode.
- The TV remembers the last caption/text mode selected, even after the TV is turned off.,['Manual35_36', 'Manual35_37', 'Manual35_38']

For optimal over-the-air reception, an outdoor antenna is recommended. Before connecting it, please inspect the antenna and cable carefully: if the antenna is badly weathered, the cable is cracked, loose, corroded, or damaged, signal quality may be reduced.

<PIC>

1. Connect the outdoor antenna to the TV

Choose the connection method that matches your antenna cable:

• If your antenna uses 300 ohm flat wire:
Connect the two ends of the 300 ohm flat wire to the screws on a 300 ohm to 75 ohm adapter. Tighten the screws securely, then insert the adapter into the TV’s 75 ohm antenna jack.

• If your antenna uses a 75 ohm coaxial cable:
Connect the 75 ohm coaxial cable directly to the TV’s 75 ohm antenna jack. Make sure the connector is firmly seated so the signal is stable.

2. Confirm the TV is ready

After the antenna is connected, check that:

• The TV is connected to the antenna or cable system.
• The TV power cord is plugged into a working power outlet.
• The antenna is positioned as high and clear of obstructions as possible for better reception.

3. Run Auto Program to store available channels

AUTO PROGRAM searches for active channels in your area and saves them so you can select them with the channel buttons.

Using the AUTO PRG. button:

1. Press the AUTO PRG. button on the remote control.
2. Press the right arrow button or press AUTO PRG. again to start channel searching.

<PIC>

The TV will begin scanning and memorizing receivable channels.

<PIC>

If you need to stop the scan before it finishes, press the ENTER button. Only the channels found up to that point will be saved.

4. Improve reception if the picture is poor

If the signal is weak, snowy, doubled, or unstable after Auto Program:

• Adjust the antenna direction or position, then run AUTO PROGRAM again.
• Move the antenna away from large metal objects or sources of interference when possible.
• If some channels are still unclear, use the Fine adjustment function to tune the picture, then press ENTER to save the adjustment.
• If the signal quality remains poor, check the antenna cable and connections again, as damaged or loose cabling can cause reception problems.

For the best result, make small antenna position changes and rescan channels after each major adjustment.,['Manual35_39', 'Manual35_5', 'Manual35_7']

For the best performance, connect the DVD player to the TV using the component DVD input connections. Component video separates the picture signal into Y, PB, and PR, which provides better image quality than a standard composite video connection.

1. Turn off the TV and DVD player before connecting the cables.

2. Connect the DVD player’s component video outputs to the TV’s COMPONENT DVD INPUT (480i) jacks:
   - DVD Y output → TV Y input
   - DVD PB output → TV PB input
   - DVD PR output → TV PR input

3. Connect the DVD player’s audio outputs to the TV’s audio input jacks:
   - DVD L audio output → TV L audio input
   - DVD R audio output → TV R audio input

<PIC>

4. If the DVD player uses different labels for the component video outputs, match them as follows:
   - Y connects to Y
   - Cb / B-Y / Pb / PB connects to PB
   - Cr / R-Y / Pr / PR connects to PR

<PIC>

5. Turn on the TV, then turn on the DVD player.

6. Press the TV/VIDEO button on the remote control, or the TV/VIDEO button on the TV front panel, until COMPONENT is selected.

7. Start playback on the DVD player. The DVD picture and sound should now play through the TV.

Note: The TV remembers the last selected input mode, even after the TV is turned off. If the DVD picture does not appear the next time you use it, press TV/VIDEO again and select COMPONENT.,['Manual35_43', 'Manual35_44']

During pressure cooking, please follow these safety precautions to ensure safe operation throughout the process:

<PIC>

1. Pre-heating stage
During pre-heating, the cooker vaporizes liquid to create steam. As pressure builds, the float valve rises and locks the lid in place. This is normal. The pre-heating time may be longer when cooking frozen food or when the inner pot contains a larger amount of food and liquid.

<PIC>

2. Cooking stage
Once the required pressure is reached, the cooking countdown begins. The cooker will automatically maintain the selected pressure level. Keep hands, face, and exposed skin away from the steam release area at all times, as hot steam may be released.

<PIC>

3. Depressurization stage
After cooking finishes, the cooker may still be hot and pressurized. Pressure must be reduced before the lid can be opened safely. Follow the recipe’s depressurization method and use proper venting techniques.

Important safety warnings:

DANGER: Do not attempt to remove the lid while the float valve is up, and never force the lid open. The contents are under extreme pressure. The float valve must be down before you attempt to remove the lid. Failure to follow this may result in serious personal injury and/or property damage.

WARNING: Steam released from the steam release valve is very hot. Do not place hands, face, or any exposed skin over the steam release valve when releasing pressure. Do not cover the steam release valve.

NOTICE: Do not use Quick Release when cooking fatty, oily, thick, or high-starch foods, such as stews, chilis, pasta, and congee. Also avoid Quick Release for foods that expand during cooking, such as beans and grains, as this may cause food or liquid to spray from the valve.

In short, always wait until pressure has fully dropped, confirm that the float valve is down, and only then open the lid carefully.,['Manual30_6', 'Manual30_7', 'Manual30_8']

Your electric toothbrush has 3 intensity settings, so you can choose the brushing strength that feels most comfortable for your oral care needs:

- High intensity: three lights illuminated, for a stronger clean
- Medium intensity: two lights illuminated, for a balanced everyday clean
- Low intensity: one light illuminated, for a gentler brushing experience

<PIC>

To adjust the intensity manually, press the intensity indicator area on the toothbrush handle. Each press cycles through the available intensity levels, so you can select the setting you prefer. You can change the intensity before brushing, while brushing, or after brushing.

<PIC>

You can also customize the intensity setting in the app. The app lets you adjust toothbrush preferences such as intensity settings, mode controls, Adaptive Intensity, and brushing feedback.

Please note: If Adaptive Intensity is active and you apply too much pressure for an extended period, the toothbrush will automatically lower the intensity by 1 level to help protect your teeth and gums. At the start of each new brushing cycle, the toothbrush will return to your pre-selected intensity setting.,['toothbrush0_06', 'toothbrush0_07']

You can customize your electric toothbrush by adjusting the brushing intensity, changing modes in the app, and activating or deactivating supported smart features.

For the most complete customization experience, use the app. You can customize:
- Intensity settings
- Mode controls
- Toothbrush feedback
- Adaptive Intensity on/off
- Scrubbing Feedback on/off

To set up customization in the app:
1. Download the app to your phone.
2. Make sure Bluetooth is turned on.
3. Pick up the toothbrush so it becomes active and the lights turn on.
4. Open the app and follow the guided setup steps.
5. Pair your toothbrush with the app.
6. Create your account in the app. If prompted, complete the firmware update to access the latest features.
7. Brush regularly and sync the toothbrush with the app regularly so your settings and brushing data stay up to date.

You can also manually change the intensity directly on the toothbrush handle. The toothbrush has 3 intensity levels:
- High intensity: 3 lights
- Medium intensity: 2 lights
- Low intensity: 1 light

<PIC>

To select the intensity manually, press the intensity indicator area on the handle to cycle through the available levels. You can change the intensity before brushing, during brushing, or after brushing.

<PIC>

You can activate or deactivate these features from the handle:
- Adaptive Intensity
- Pressure Sensor Feedback
- Brush head replacement reminder

Please note: if Pressure Sensor Feedback is deactivated, Adaptive Intensity will also be deactivated.

To activate or deactivate a feature from the handle:
1. Place the toothbrush handle on the charging stand.
2. Press and hold the power button for the required amount of time, depending on the feature you want to change.

<PIC>

Use the timing and confirmation indicators below:

Adaptive Intensity:
- Press and hold the power button for up to 3 seconds.
- You will hear 1 beep.
- The SenseIQ indicator will light up for 3 seconds.

Pressure Sensor Feedback:
- Press and hold the power button for up to 5 seconds.
- You will hear 1 beep, followed by 2 beeps.
- The SenseIQ indicator and the light ring will light up purple for 3 seconds.

Brush head replacement reminder:
- Press and hold the power button for up to 7 seconds.
- You will hear 1 beep, then 2 beeps, then 3 beeps.
- The brush head replacement reminder indicator will light up for 3 seconds.

<PIC>

After changing a setting, check the battery indicator and tones for confirmation:
- Activated: the battery indicator blinks white 3 times, and you hear 3 tones from low to high.
- Deactivated: the battery indicator blinks amber 3 times, and you hear 3 tones from high to low.,['toothbrush0_06', 'toothbrush0_07', 'Manual37_13', 'toothbrush0_11']

4. Check that charging has started correctly. The toothbrush should beep twice, and the lights on the handle should illuminate in an upward motion. During charging, the battery indicator will blink white.
